stem: Muscle not supplied by Ulnar nerve is:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Opponens Pollicis is supplied by the median nerve (recurrent branch), not the ulnar nerve.
• It allows opposition of the thumb (touching thumb to other fingers). Lesion of median nerve -> Inability to oppose thumb (ape thumb deformity).

Other Options:
Option B: Abductor digiti minimi brevis
Supplied by the deep branch of ulnar nerve. Abducts the little finger. Important in hypothenar muscle group testing.
Option C: 4th Lumbrical
Medial two lumbricals (3rd & 4th) are supplied by the ulnar nerve. They flex MCP joints and extend IP joints. Damage causes clawing of ring and little finger.
Option D: Dorsal Interossei
All interossei are supplied by the deep branch of ulnar nerve. Dorsal interossei abduct fingers (DAB rule). Paralysis causes loss of finger spreading.

stem: Action of the marked muscle is:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The image marks the lateral pterygoid muscle, which is the only muscle of mastication that depresses the mandible (opens the mouth).
• It pulls the mandibular condyle and disc forward onto the articular tubercle.
• This makes it a key muscle in jaw opening.

Other Options:
Option A: Retraction
Retraction is performed mainly by the posterior fibers of temporalis and partly by deep fibers of masseter. Lateral pterygoid does not retract the mandible.
Option C: Elevation
Elevation is done by masseter, temporalis and medial pterygoid. These muscles close the mouth and generate bite force, opposite to lateral pterygoid action.
Option D: Protrusion
Both lateral and medial pterygoids contribute to forward movement of the mandible, but the specific primary action here is depression.

stem: Action of muscle attached to lower part of sacrum:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The piriformis muscle externally rotates the thigh at the hip joint. It also assists in abduction of the flexed thigh.
• Clinically important in piriformis syndrome, where the sciatic nerve is compressed as it passes below the muscle.

Other Options:
Option B: Hip flexion
Hip flexion is chiefly performed by iliopsoas (psoas major + iliacus), along with sartorius and rectus femoris. Piriformis does not flex the hip.
Option C: Knee flexion
Knee flexion is done by the hamstrings (biceps femoris, semitendinosus, semimembranosus) and assisted by gastrocnemius. Piriformis has no action at the knee.
Option D: Knee extension
Knee extension is carried out by the quadriceps femoris group (rectus femoris, vasti). Piriformis does not extend the knee.

stem: Desmosomes are present in which part of cell:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Desmosomes are specialized intercellular junctions located mainly at the apicolateral region of epithelial cells, providing strong adhesion between adjacent cells.
• They connect intermediate filaments of the cytoskeleton, giving mechanical strength to tissues such as skin and mucosa.

Other Options:
Option A: Apical
Apical region mainly has tight junctions (zonula occludens) and microvilli structures. Desmosomes are not located purely at the apical region.
Option B: Basal
Basal surface has hemidesmosomes, which anchor epithelial cells to the basement membrane, not desmosomes.
Option D: Basolateral
Basolateral region mainly has gap junctions and adherens junctions that allow communication and mechanical stability.

stem: Hepato-cystic triangle-boundaries are formed by all except?
subject: Anatomy, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The cystic artery is not a boundary of the hepatocystic triangle; it is usually found within the triangle.
• The triangle is also known as Calot's triangle.
• Its classical boundaries are: cystic duct (inferiorly), common hepatic duct (medially) and inferior surface of liver (superiorly).
• The cystic artery arises from the right hepatic artery and traverses the triangle to supply the gallbladder.
• This triangle is of high surgical importance in cholecystectomy to avoid accidental bile duct or vascular injury.

Other Options:
Option A: Cystic duct
Cystic duct forms the inferior boundary of the hepatocystic triangle.
Option B: Hepatic duct
Hepatic duct (common hepatic duct) forms the medial boundary of the triangle.
Option C: Inferior border of liver
Inferior border of liver forms the superior boundary of the triangle.

stem: Which is Supplied by Superficial Peroneal nerve?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The superficial peroneal (fibular) nerve supplies the muscles of the lateral compartment of leg: peroneus longus and peroneus brevis.
• Peroneus longus everts the foot and supports the transverse arch of the foot. Nerve root supply is mainly L5-S1.
• Injury to superficial peroneal nerve leads to loss of eversion and sensory loss over the dorsum of foot (except first web space).
• Peroneus tertius and FHL are not innervated by this nerve.

Other Options:
Option B: Peroneus Brevis
Peroneus Brevis is also supplied by the superficial peroneal nerve. It everts the foot and supports the lateral longitudinal arch. (Note: While technically supplied, Peroneus Longus is the primary answer in this specific MCQ context).
Option C: Peroneus Tertius
Peroneus Tertius is supplied by the deep peroneal nerve, not superficial. Assists in dorsiflexion and eversion of foot.
Option D: FHL
FHL (Flexor Hallucis Longus) is supplied by the tibial nerve in the posterior compartment of leg.

stem: Which nerve does not help in Olfaction?
subject: Anatomy, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) is a motor nerve that supplies all intrinsic and extrinsic muscles of the tongue (except palatoglossus, supplied by vagus).
• It has no sensory or special sensory component and therefore does not contribute to smell or taste.
• Lesion -> tongue deviation to the side of lesion, but no olfactory deficit.
• This makes it the only option listed that does not play any role in olfaction.

Other Options:
Option B: Glossopharyngeal
Glossopharyngeal provides taste sensation from the posterior one-third of tongue. Also carries visceral sensation from pharynx and contributes to the gag reflex, indirectly associated with flavor perception.
Option C: Trigeminal
Trigeminal provides general sensory innervation to the nasal mucosa. Detects irritant/chemical stimuli (e.g. ammonia), which is part of the perception of smell.
Option D: Vagus
Vagus provides sensation to parts of the pharynx, larynx, and epiglottis. Some taste fibers from the epiglottis travel via vagus, contributing indirectly to flavor perception.

stem: Cleft Lip - Primary muscle to be repaired:
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The orbicularis oris is the principal muscle repaired in cleft lip surgery.
• In cleft lip, the fibers of orbicularis oris are abnormally inserted into the skin of the lip or nasal base, instead of forming a continuous sphincter.
• Proper re-approximation restores lip competence, symmetry and function.
• Repair is usually done at 10 weeks of age (Rule of 10: 10 weeks, 10 pounds, 10 g/dl Hb).

Other Options:
Option B: Orbicularis oculi
Orbicularis oculi is the muscle around the eye, responsible for eyelid closure. Not involved in cleft lip deformity or repair.
Option C: Zygomaticus major
Zygomaticus major is a muscle of facial expression that elevates the angle of the mouth (smiling). Not surgically corrected in cleft lip procedures.
Option D: Levator labii superioris
Levator labii superioris elevates the upper lip. While it may be affected secondarily, the primary target in cleft lip repair is always orbicularis oris.

stem: Fish bone stuck in Piriform fossa, Which Structure likely to be damaged?
subject: Anatomy, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• The internal laryngeal nerve (ILN), a branch of the superior laryngeal nerve, pierces the thyrohyoid membrane to provide sensory innervation to the mucosa of piriform fossa and laryngeal inlet.
• A sharp object such as a fish bone can damage this nerve while lodged in the piriform fossa.
• Damage leads to loss of sensation in the mucosa above the vocal cords and the piriform fossa. This can predispose to silent aspiration due to impaired protective reflex.

Other Options:
Option B: RLN
Recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN) supplies all intrinsic muscles of larynx except cricothyroid. Lies lower, near tracheoesophageal groove, not directly at risk in piriform fossa foreign bodies.
Option C: SLNh
Superior laryngeal nerve (SLN) has two branches: external (motor) and internal (sensory). Only the internal branch is endangered in piriform fossa.
Option D: ELN
External laryngeal nerve (ELN) is a motor nerve to the cricothyroid muscle. Runs close to superior thyroid artery, not piriform fossa.

stem: Infection of Duramater. Pain is referred to:
options: Face around the orbit and upper nose
subject: Anatomy, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The supratentorial dura mater is innervated mainly by the trigeminal nerve (ophthalmic division - V1).
• Pain arising from this dura is therefore referred to areas supplied by V1, which includes the forehead and anterior scalp.
• Hence, inflammation or infection of the dura mater commonly presents as frontal headache.

Other Options:
Option A: Back of the head
Posterior fossa (infratentorial dura) pain is referred via upper cervical nerves (C1-C3).
Option C: Behind the ear/mastoid region
Behind the ear/mastoid region is more suggestive of mastoiditis or ear pathology.
Option D: Face around the orbit and upper nose
Face around the orbit and upper nose may occur, but classical presentation for supratentorial dural infection is frontal headache.

stem: Cross Section of Thorax: Which of the following is incorrectly matched?
options: Receives oxygenated blood from Pulmonary veins - LA
Lies right of ascending aorta - PT
subject: Anatomy, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• The pulmonary trunk (PT) lies to the left of the ascending aorta, not right.
• In cross-sectional thoracic imaging, the PT is seen anterior and slightly to the left of the ascending aorta.
• Misplacement in such questions helps check orientation in CT/MRI slices. Correct anatomical knowledge is essential for interpreting radiology.

Other Options:
Option A: Receives oxygenated blood from Pulmonary veins - LA
The left atrium (LA) receives oxygenated blood from 4 pulmonary veins. Seen posteriorly in cross-sections.
Option C: Arises from LV - Aorta
The aorta arises from the left ventricle and carries oxygenated blood to systemic circulation.
Option D: Hypertrophy in pulmonary stenosis - RV
Pulmonary stenosis increases resistance to blood flow out of the right ventricle (RV), leading to RV hypertrophy.

stem: Carbon monoxide (CO) and cyanide (CN) lead to death because they inhibit which of the following proteins/enzymes?
subject: Biochemistry, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Cytochrome oxidase (Complex IV) is the terminal enzyme of the electron transport chain (ETC).
• Cyanide and carbon monoxide both bind to the heme a3-CuB center of Complex IV.
• This prevents the reduction of oxygen to water, effectively blocking oxidative phosphorylation. As a result, ATP production halts, leading to cellular hypoxia despite normal oxygen levels in blood.

Other Options:
Option A: Myoglobin
Myoglobin is an intracellular oxygen-binding protein in muscle. It is not inhibited by CO or CN in lethal doses.
Option B: Hemoglobin
Hemoglobin binds CO strongly (forming carboxyhemoglobin) and reduces oxygen delivery, but cellular death is fundamentally due to ETC inhibition at Complex IV, not hemoglobin binding alone.
Option D: NADH dehydrogenase (Complex 1)
NADH dehydrogenase (Complex 1) is inhibited by rotenone, amytal, piericidin A. Not the target of CO or CN poisoning.

stem:
Match the following:
Column AColumn B
1. Northern blottingA. Protein identification
2. Southern blottingB. RNA Identification
3. Eastern blottingC. DNA identification
4. Western blottingD. Post-translational modifications of proteins
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Northern blotting detects RNA by hybridization with complementary DNA/RNA probes.
• Southern blotting identifies DNA fragments, developed by Edwin Southern.
• Eastern blotting studies post-translational modifications like glycosylation/lipidation on proteins.
• Western blotting detects specific proteins using antibodies (clinical test for HIV confirmation).
• These four blotting techniques are classic molecular biology tools and often asked in exams.

Other Options:
Option B: 1-C, 2-B, 3-E, 4-D
Wrong because it assigns Northern blot to DNA, which is incorrect.
Option C: 1-B, 2-C, 3-A, 4-D
Wrong because it confuses Eastern blotting with protein detection, which is Western blot's role.
Option D: 1-C, 2-B, 3-D, 4-A
Wrong because it mismatches Northern with DNA and mixes up protein identification.

stem: A patient presented with hepatosplenomegaly and hypoglycaemia. Glucagon administration shows improvement in blood glucose levels after a meal. However, there is no improvement in blood glucose levels after an overnight fast followed by glucagon administration. What is the most likely enzyme deficiency?
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency -> Von Gierke's disease (GSD Type 1).
• Liver cannot convert glucose-6-phosphate into free glucose, hence severe fasting hypoglycemia.
• Patients present with hepatomegaly due to glycogen accumulation.
• Glucagon has no effect in fasting since the pathway is blocked at the final step (release of free glucose from the liver).
• Other features: lactic acidosis, hyperuricemia, hyperlipidemia.

Other Options:
Option A: Debranching enzyme
Debranching enzyme deficiency -> Cori's disease (GSD Type III). Hypoglycemia is milder; glucagon still raises glucose.
Option B: Glycogen phosphorylase
Glycogen phosphorylase deficiency in liver -> Hers disease (GSD Type VI). Patients usually respond to glucagon.
Option C: Muscle phosphorylase
Muscle phosphorylase deficiency -> McArdle's disease (GSD Type V). Affects skeletal muscle, not liver; no hypoglycemia.

stem: After how many cycles of PCR duplication will the result become half of that obtained after 26 cycles?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• PCR follows exponential amplification: number of DNA molecules = 2^n (where n = cycles).
• After 26 cycles amplification = 2^26. Half of that = 2^25, which corresponds to 25 cycles.
• This shows how exponential growth halves with one fewer cycle.

Other Options:
Option B: 27
27 cycles = 2^27 which is double, not half of 2^26.
Option C: 17
17 cycles is far less than half.
Option D: 10
10 cycles is negligible compared to 2^26.

stem: Which of the following will not be able to take up glucose in absence of insulin?
subject: Biochemistry, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Skeletal muscle glucose uptake is mediated by GLUT-4 transporters, which are insulin-dependent.
• In absence of insulin (e.g. Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus), skeletal muscle cannot take up glucose effectively, leading to hyperglycemia and muscle wasting.
• Other tissues (brain, RBCs, kidney) utilize insulin-independent transporters, so they continue glucose uptake.

Other Options:
Option B: RBC
RBCs use GLUT-1 transporters, which are insulin-independent.
Option C: Brain
Brain uses GLUT-3 transporters, high-affinity and insulin-independent.
Option D: Kidney
Kidney proximal tubule reabsorption uses GLUT-2 transporters, also insulin-independent.

stem: A patient presents with hepatosplenomegaly and a cherry red spot at the macula. Which enzyme deficiency is most likely?
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Niemann-Pick disease (Types A & B) results from sphingomyelinase deficiency.
• Leads to accumulation of sphingomyelin in liver, spleen, brain -> hepatosplenomegaly + neurodegeneration.
• Classic ocular finding = cherry red spot at macula.
• Distinguishing clue from Tay-Sachs is the presence of hepatosplenomegaly.

Other Options:
Option B: Hexosaminidase A
Hexosaminidase A deficiency -> Tay-Sachs disease. Also has a cherry red spot, but no hepatosplenomegaly.
Option C: Glucocerebrosidase
Glucocerebrosidase deficiency -> Gaucher's disease. Causes hepatosplenomegaly, bone pain, "crumpled tissue paper" macrophages, but no cherry red spot.
Option D: a-Galactosidase A
a-Galactosidase A deficiency -> Fabry's disease (X-linked). Features angiokeratomas, peripheral neuropathy, renal failure. No cherry red spot.

stem: Two different specimens from two patients are received. Which method will help confirm they are from different individuals?
subject: Biochemistry, FMT
explanation:
Solution
• Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism (RFLP) detects differences in DNA sequences between individuals.
• It was the classical method of DNA fingerprinting for identity testing, forensic cases and paternity disputes.
• Different individuals show unique RFLP patterns. Hence, it is the best method among the options for confirming samples from different individuals.

Other Options:
Option B: Mutation analysis
Mutation analysis detects specific mutations in a gene. Not suitable for identity testing.
Option C: Pyrosequencing
Pyrosequencing is a sequencing technique more useful in mutation detection, SNP analysis, not primarily for identity discrimination.
Option D: Karyotyping
Karyotyping detects chromosomal abnormalities. Cannot differentiate between two normal individuals.

stem: The term "gene pool" refers to:
options: The total number of genes present in an individual
The total number of alleles present in a population
The number of dominant genes in a species
The number of chromosomes in an organism
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• A gene pool includes all alleles of all genes carried by individuals in a population. It represents the genetic variability of a population.
• Large gene pools -> greater genetic diversity and adaptability.
• Small gene pools -> inbreeding, increased risk of genetic disorders.

Other Options:
Option A: The total number of genes present in an individual
Total number of genes in an individual refers to the genome of an individual, not a population.
Option C: The number of dominant genes in a species
Number of dominant genes is incorrect; dominance/recessiveness is not how gene pools are defined.
Option D: The number of chromosomes in an organism
Number of chromosomes refers to the karyotype (species-specific), not the gene pool.

stem: A newborn is diagnosed with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency. Which of the following types of bilirubin is most likely to be elevated in this condition?
subject: Pediatrics, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• G6PD deficiency -> reduced NADPH and reduced glutathione. RBCs become vulnerable to oxidative damage -> hemolysis.
• Hemolysis releases heme which is broken down to unconjugated bilirubin.
• Liver conjugation capacity is overwhelmed, so unconjugated bilirubin accumulates in blood.
• Leads to neonatal jaundice and, if severe, kernicterus.

Other Options:
Option A: Conjugated bilirubin
Conjugated bilirubin is elevated in obstructive jaundice (e.g. biliary atresia), not in hemolytic conditions.
Option C: Both conjugated and unconjugated bilirubin
Both conjugated and unconjugated bilirubin are seen in hepatocellular jaundice (mixed pattern).
Option D: Urobilinogen
Urobilinogen may be increased in hemolysis, but the main blood bilirubin rise is unconjugated.

stem: Phase 1 detoxification mainly involves:
subject: Biochemistry, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Phase I detoxification introduces or unmasks a polar functional group (-OH, -NH2, -COOH).
• Most common reaction is oxidation, catalyzed by cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP450 system) in liver microsomes.
• Makes xenobiotics more hydrophilic and prepares them for Phase II conjugation.

Other Options:
Option B: Reduction
Reduction is a Phase I reaction but relatively less common compared to oxidation.
Option C: Hydrolysis
Hydrolysis is also a Phase I reaction (e.g., esterases), but oxidation is the primary mechanism.
Option D: Conjugation
Conjugation is Phase II detoxification (e.g. glucuronidation, sulfation), not Phase 1.

stem: Which of the following is seen at high altitude?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• At high altitude, low barometric pressure causes hypobaric hypoxia.
• Hypoxia stimulates peripheral chemoreceptors -> increases ventilation (hyperventilation).
• Hyperventilation causes excess CO2 washout, reducing arterial pCO2.
• This leads to respiratory alkalosis.
• Over 2-3 days, renal compensation occurs by excreting bicarbonate, restoring pH closer to normal.

Other Options:
Option A: Respiratory acidosis
Respiratory acidosis is seen in hypoventilation, not high altitude.
Option C: Metabolic acidosis
Metabolic acidosis is caused by accumulation of metabolic acids.
Option D: Metabolic alkalosis
Metabolic alkalosis is seen in vomiting or diuretic use.

stem: Surface tension in alveoli changes/increases during:
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• During inspiration, alveoli enlarge and surfactant molecules spread over a larger surface area.
• This reduces the effectiveness of surfactant momentarily, and surface tension rises.
• During expiration, alveoli shrink and surfactant molecules become more concentrated, preventing alveolar collapse by reducing surface tension.

Other Options:
Option B: Expiration
Expiration - Surface tension decreases as alveoli shrink.
Option C: End-inspiration
End-inspiration - Surface tension is high, but the critical rise occurs during inspiration itself.
Option D: End-expiration
End-expiration - Surface tension is at its lowest due to maximum surfactant concentration.

stem: Which of the following is an example of feedforward regulation?
options: HR Increase upon supine to standing
HR Increase before starting exercise (anticipatory tachycardia)
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Feedforward regulation is anticipatory homeostatic control.
• Example: HR and ventilation increase before muscle activity starts -> mediated by CNS anticipation, not by feedback from exercise metabolites.
• It helps minimize the lag in physiological adjustments.
• Thus, anticipatory tachycardia is the classic example.

Other Options:
Option A: HR Increase upon supine to standing
HR Increase upon supine to standing is feedback (baroreceptor reflex).
Option B: Vasoconstriction upon exposure to cold
Vasoconstriction upon exposure to cold is also feedback.
Option D: Salivation on seeing food
Salivation on seeing food is a conditioned reflex (Pavlovian), not true feedforward homeostatic regulation.

stem: IGF-1 and IGF-2 are structurally similar to:
subject: Biochemistry, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• IGF-1 and IGF-2 are peptide hormones secreted mainly by the liver under the influence of growth hormone.
• They share ~50% sequence homology with Insulin.
• Both have alpha and beta chains linked by disulfide bonds, similar to insulin's structure.
• Their major role is mediating anabolic and growth effects of GH.

Other Options:
Option A: Preproinsulin
Preproinsulin contains a signal peptide for entry into ER, not structurally related to IGFs.
Option B: Proinsulin
Proinsulin contains insulin + C-peptide. Not homologous to IGFs.
Option D: C-peptide
C-peptide is a useful marker of endogenous insulin secretion, not structurally similar to IGFs.

stem: If contraction period is 40 msec, calculate the tetanizing frequency:
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Tetanus occurs when stimuli are given so frequently that individual contractions fuse.
• The minimal frequency required to produce tetanus is called tetanizing frequency. Formula = 1 / contraction period (in sec).
• Here: contraction period = 40 msec = 0.04 sec. 1 / 0.04 = 25/sec.
• Tetanus allows muscles to generate sustained force, critical for normal movements.

Other Options:
Option B: 40/sec
• This would correspond to a contraction period of 25 msec, not 40 msec.
Option C: 45/sec
Higher than required; actual calculation gives 25/sec.
Option D: 50/sec
• Would correspond to contraction period of 20 msec.

stem: Patient was asked to open the eyes then to close. Which wave will be recorded on EEG?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Alpha waves (8-13 Hz) are best seen in the occipital region when the person is awake, relaxed and has eyes closed.
• Opening the eyes or mental activity suppresses alpha waves and replaces them with beta waves - this is called alpha block (Berger's effect).
• Thus, during the 'open close' sequence, the wave most clearly recorded is alpha.
• Clinically useful in evaluating consciousness and cortical function.
• EEG changes are key in sleep physiology and neurological disorders.

Other Options:
Option B: Beta
Seen with eyes open, active thinking and alertness.
Option C: Theta
Normal in children; in adults indicates drowsiness or light sleep.
Option D: Delta
• Seen in deep sleep (slow-wave sleep) or pathological states (encephalopathy).

stem: Patient has come to hospital OPD with fever. Which hypothalamic area is involved?
subject: Physiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The preoptic area of hypothalamus contains the body's 'thermostat'.
• Pyrogens (IL-1, TNF, IL-6) stimulate PGE2 synthesis, which raises the set point.
• This results in fever (increased heat production, decreased heat loss).
• Antipyretics (NSAIDs, paracetamol) reduce fever by blocking prostaglandin synthesis in this region.
• Hence, fever is mediated by the preoptic nucleus of the hypothalamus.

Other Options:
Option B: Paraventricular nucleus
• Secretes oxytocin and ADH; also involved in stress response via CRH. Not temperature regulation.
Option C: Medial hypothalamus
• Contains the satiety center, regulating food intake. Not linked with fever.
Option D: Lateral hypothalamus
• Feeding center; stimulation causes hunger. Lesion -> anorexia. Not involved in thermoregulation.

stem: A patient on anti epileptic drug Carbamazepine presented with ataxia. History reveals he was recently started on Erythromycin. What is the most likely cause:
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Carbamazepine is metabolized in the liver by CYP3A4 enzyme.
• Erythromycin is a CYP3A4 inhibitor, so it decreases the metabolism of carbamazepine.
• This results in raised plasma levels of carbamazepine -> neurotoxicity (ataxia, diplopia, nystagmus, sedation). The clinical presentation matches carbamazepine toxicity.
• Management: dose adjustment or avoidance of the drug combination.

Other Options:
Option A: Erythromycin toxicity
• Erythromycin itself can cause GI upset, QT prolongation and cholestasis, but ataxia is not a feature.
Option C: Worsened epileptic disorder
• If carbamazepine metabolism were induced, seizures could worsen. Here the problem is inhibition -> toxicity, not worsening epilepsy.
Option D: Decreased action of carbamazepine
• This occurs with enzyme inducers (e.g. phenytoin, rifampicin, phenobarbital), not with inhibitors like erythromycin.

stem:
Which pharmacokinetic parameter primarily determines the loading dose of a drug?
1. Volume of distribution (Vd)
2. Half-life
3. Plasma concentration
4. Bioavailability
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Loading dose is the initial higher dose given to quickly achieve therapeutic plasma concentration.
• Determined by:
1. Vd (Volume of distribution): how widely the drug distributes.
2. Cp (Plasma concentration): desired therapeutic concentration.
3. F (Bioavailability): fraction of drug reaching systemic circulation (important in oral drugs).
• Half-life affects how frequently the drug should be given (maintenance dose), but not the loading dose calculation.

Other Options:
Option A: 1 and 2 only
Wrong because half-life does not determine loading dose.
Option C: 2 and 4 only
Wrong because Vd and plasma concentration are missing.
Option D: 3 and 4 only
Wrong because Vd is also a key factor.

stem: A 24-year-old sexually active male presents to the outpatient department with complaints of painful urination and a thick yellow urethral discharge for the past 3 days. He denies any fever, chills or flank pain. He reports multiple unprotected sexual encounters in the past month. A urethral swab is sent for Gram stain, which shows intracellular gram-negative diplococci within polymorphonuclear leukocytes. Nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) confirms the diagnosis. He was previously treated with penicillin, but the symptoms persisted. You suspect antibiotic resistance. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Neisseria gonorrhoeae is highly resistant to penicillin due to plasmid-mediated beta-lactamase.
• Ceftriaxone (IM, single dose) is currently the drug of choice.
• Often combined with azithromycin or doxycycline to cover possible Chlamydia trachomatis co-infection.
• Vancomycin is not used (effective against gram-positives).
• Amoxicillin is ineffective due to resistance.
• Tetracyclines are not the primary drug for gonorrhea (used for chlamydia).

Other Options:
Option B: Vancomycin
• Acts on gram-positive organisms (e.g., MRSA, enterococci), not N. gonorrhoeae.
Option C: Amoxicillin
• Not effective due to widespread penicillin resistance in N. gonorrhoeae.
Option D: Tetracycline
• Useful in Chlamydia trachomatis infection but not the primary treatment for gonorrhea.

stem: A 62-year-old male with a history of coronary heart disease (CHD) and renal failure was admitted to the ICU with elevated blood pressure (BP 190/110 mmHg) and declining urine output. The intensivist administers Fenoldopam intravenously. How does this drug help in improving renal function in this patient?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Fenoldopam is a selective dopamine D1 receptor agonist.
• D1 stimulation in renal arteries -> vasodilation -> increased renal blood flow.
• Promotes natriuresis and diuresis, helping improve urine output in renal failure.
• Also lowers blood pressure via systemic vasodilation; useful in hypertensive emergencies.
• Advantage: preserves renal perfusion while reducing BP, unlike many other vasodilators.

Other Options:
Option A: D1 antagonism
Would block renal vasodilation and worsen renal perfusion.
Option B: D2 antagonism
• Not the mechanism of Fenoldopam; D2 antagonists (e.g., antipsychotics) act in CNS.
Option C: Alpha antagonism
• Alpha blockers reduce BP via vasodilation, but do not selectively increase renal perfusion like Fenoldopam.

stem: Which of the following drugs is used to reverse the effects of Vecuronium?
subject: Pharmacology, Anesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• Vecuronium competitively blocks nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction.
• Neostigmine, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, prevents the breakdown of acetylcholine.
• This increases ACh levels, which outcompetes vecuronium and reverses paralysis.
• Often co-administered with Atropine or Glycopyrrolate to prevent muscarinic side effects (bradycardia, secretions).
• Thus, Neostigmine is the reversal drug for vecuronium.

Other Options:
Option A: Atropine
• Muscarinic antagonist.
• Not used alone; given with neostigmine to reduce muscarinic adverse effects.
Option C: Baclofen
GABA B agonist, used for spasticity.
• No role in neuromuscular block reversal.
Option D: Dantrolene sodium
Inhibits Ca2+ release from sarcoplasmic reticulum.
• Used in malignant hyperthermia and neuroleptic malignant syndrome, not vecuronium reversal.

stem:
A 4-month-old infant is brought to the dermatology OPD with intense itching, especially at night. On examination, there are multiple excoriated papules and burrows seen over the palms, soles and trunk. A clinical diagnosis of scabies is made. The pediatrician plans to initiate topical therapy. Which of the following drugs are appropriate and safe for use in infants with scabies?
1. Permethrin 5% cream
2. Benzyl benzoate 25% lotion
3. Crotamiton 10% cream
4. Oral Ivermectin
subject: Pharmacology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Permethrin 5% cream is the first-line treatment for scabies, FDA-approved and safe for infants >= 2 months.
• Crotamiton 10% cream can be used; though less effective than permethrin, it is safe for infants.
• Benzyl benzoate 25% lotion is too irritant and contraindicated in infants.
• Oral Ivermectin is reserved for resistant cases and not used in infants <15 kg.

Other Options:
Option A: 1 and 2 only
Incorrect, since benzyl benzoate is unsafe in infants.
Option B: 1, 3 and 4
Incorrect, ivermectin is contraindicated in infants <15 kg.
Option D: 2 and 4 only
• Wrong, both benzyl benzoate and ivermectin are unsafe here.

stem:
Match the following drugs with their respective Schedules under the Drugs and Cosmetics Rules, 1945:
DrugsSchedule
A. Hepatitis B vaccine1. Schedule X
B. Veterinary drugs2. Schedule H
C. Morphine3. Schedule G
D. Insulin4. Schedule Z
subject: Pharmacology, FMT
explanation:
Solution
• Hepatitis B vaccine is biological, comes under Schedule C, but matched as 3 (Schedule G) in the forced options.
• Veterinary drugs -> Schedule H (prescription-only).
• Morphine -> Schedule X (stringent control due to addiction potential).
• Insulin -> Schedule G (caution: taken only under medical supervision).
• Thus, the closest correct mapping is given in Option D.

Other Options:
Option A: Wrong, mixes Morphine with Schedule H.
Option B: Wrong, misplaces Insulin under Schedule H.
Option C: Wrong, misplaces Morphine into Schedule G.

stem: Which of the following statements regarding Thalidomide is FALSE?
options: It is used in type 2 lepra reaction (ENL)
It is used in relapsing multiple myeloma
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Thalidomide is an immunomodulatory drug with anti-TNF-alpha and anti-angiogenic properties, not an antimetabolite.
• It is teratogenic, producing limb deformities (phocomelia).
• Clinically used in erythema nodosum leprosum (ENL) to reduce inflammation.
• Approved for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma, usually in combination with dexamethasone or lenalidomide.
• Hence, 'antimetabolite action' is the false statement.

Other Options:
Option B: It is a teratogenic drug
• True; teratogenicity is the reason for restricted use under strict pregnancy prevention programs.
Option C: It is used in type 2 lepra reaction (ENL)
• True; reduces TNF-alpha and inflammatory cytokines in lepra reactions.
Option D: It is used in relapsing multiple myeloma
• True; FDA-approved indication.

stem:
Identify the adverse effects of Topical corticosteroids:
A. Atrophy of skin
B. Hypertrichosis
C. Acneform eruptions
D. Blue pigmentation of skin
subject: Pharmacology, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• Skin atrophy (A): Corticosteroids inhibit collagen synthesis -> thinning, striae, telangiectasia.
• Hypertrichosis (B): Excess hair growth at application site, especially with prolonged use.
• Acneform eruptions (C): Steroid acne due to follicular occlusion.
• Blue pigmentation (D): Not associated with corticosteroids (seen with drugs like minocycline, amiodarone).

Other Options:
Option A: B, C, D
Skin atrophy is a major effect and missing here.
Option C: A, B, D
Acneform eruptions are real, but pigmentation is not.
Option D: A, C, D
Hypertrichosis is a true adverse effect but missing.

stem:
Which of the following drugs are used for clinical cure of malaria?
1. Artemether
2. Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine
3. Primaquine
4. Chloroquine
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Artemether: Artemisinin derivative, potent blood schizonticide used in clinical cure.
• Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine: Antifolate combination, acts on erythrocytic schizonts -> used in clinical cure.
• Chloroquine: Classical blood schizonticide -> used in clinical cure where sensitive strains exist.
• Primaquine: Eliminates hepatic hypnozoites (P. vivax, P. ovale) -> radical cure, not clinical cure.

Other Options:
Option A: 1 and 2
Incomplete; chloroquine is also a blood schizonticide.
Option B: 1, 2 and 3
Wrong; primaquine is not for clinical cure.
Option C: 3 and 4
Wrong; primaquine is for radical cure, not clinical cure.

stem: Erythropoietin is primarily used in the treatment of which type of anemia?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• In CKD, anemia occurs due to deficiency of endogenous erythropoietin from diseased kidneys.
• Recombinant EPO restores RBC production and improves Hb levels.
• Also used in some cases of chemotherapy-induced anemia and zidovudine-induced anemia.
• Not useful in nutritional anemias (iron, folate, B12 deficiency) unless the deficiency is corrected first.
• Not first-line for aplastic anemia or MDS-related anemia.

Other Options:
Option A: Nutritional anemia
• Due to iron, folate, or vitamin B12 deficiency.
• Treated with supplementation, not erythropoietin.
Option C: Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS)-related anemia
Sometimes treated with EPO, but response is variable and not primary use.
Option D: Aplastic anemia
• Due to bone marrow failure; treated with immunosuppressants or bone marrow transplant, not EPO.

stem: Which of the following drugs is NOT used in the management of PPH?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Oxytocin: First-line drug for prevention and treatment of PPH.
• Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2alpha): Used when oxytocin fails, effective in refractory PPH.
• Misoprostol (PGE1 analog): Used as uterotonic, especially where oxytocin is unavailable.
• Dinoprostone (PGE2 analog): Primarily used for cervical ripening and induction of labor, not effective for PPH management.

Other Options:
Option A: Oxytocin
• True uterotonic; cornerstone of PPH management.
Option B: Carboprost
• Effective uterotonic for refractory cases of PPH.
Option D: Misoprostol
• Widely used alternative for PPH management, especially in low-resource settings.

stem: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding the pharmacological relationship between Imipramine and Diphenhydramine?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Imipramine (TCA) and Diphenhydramine (1st gen H1 blocker) both exhibit anticholinergic side effects -> dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, urinary retention, sedation.
• This is due to muscarinic receptor blockade.
• Their additive anticholinergic effects can worsen side effects when used together.
• Imipramine also inhibits norepinephrine/serotonin reuptake, but diphenhydramine does not.

Other Options:
Option A: Imipramine inhibits diphenhydramine's action
• No such interaction; instead, additive side effects.
Option B: Diphenhydramine inhibits imipramine's action
• Incorrect; no antagonism.
Option D: Both are norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors
• False; only TCAs like imipramine inhibit norepinephrine reuptake, diphenhydramine does not.

stem: Which of the following drugs is most teratogenic and strongly associated with neural tube defects (NTDs) when used during pregnancy?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Valproic acid:
1. Most notorious antiepileptic for NTDs (especially spina bifida).
2. Teratogenicity risk is 1-2% in exposed pregnancies.
3. Acts by inhibiting histone deacetylase and folate pathways -> impaired neural tube closure.
• Clinical advice: Women of childbearing age on valproate should receive high-dose folic acid supplementation or be shifted to safer antiepileptics (e.g. levetiracetam, lamotrigine).

Other Options:
Option A: Phenytoin
Causes fetal hydantoin syndrome (growth retardation, craniofacial anomalies, hypoplastic nails, cardiac defects).
Option B: Carbamazepine
Can also cause NTDs, but less strongly than valproic acid.
Option D: Methotrexate
• Potent teratogen (skeletal, craniofacial, limb anomalies, abortions) due to folate antagonism, but not classically NTD-specific.

stem: A 64-year-old man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes presents with fatigue and exertional dyspnea. Echo shows LV hypertrophy, LA enlargement and preserved EF (55%). BNP is 980 pg/mL. Despite optimal beta-blocker and ACE inhibitor therapy, he had two heart failure admissions in the past year. Which drug should be added to reduce future hospitalizations and improve outcomes?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• SGLT2 Inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin):
1. Now part of standard therapy for HFpEF.
2. Reduce heart failure hospitalizations, improve exercise tolerance and symptoms.
3. Benefits seen irrespective of diabetes.
• Clinical trial evidence:
1. EMPEROR-Preserved (2021) -> empagliflozin reduced risk of CV death or hospitalization in HFpEF.
2. DELIVER (2022) -> dapagliflozin showed similar benefits.

Other Options:
Option A: Digoxin
• May reduce hospitalizations in HFrEF, but not recommended for HFpEF.
Option B: Ivabradine
Only for HFrEF with EF <40%, HR >70 bpm, already on max beta-blocker.
Option C: Loop diuretic
• Useful for symptom relief (edema, dyspnea) but no mortality/hospitalization benefit.

stem: A 63-year-old diabetic woman presents with fever, flank pain, dysuria and elevated WBC count. Imaging reveals mild hydronephrosis. Urinalysis shows pyuria and bacteriuria. She is diagnosed with a complicated urinary tract infection (UTI). Which of the following is the drug of choice for empirical treatment of a complicated UTI?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Ceftriaxone (3rd gen cephalosporin) is the standard empiric drug for complicated UTI and pyelonephritis, given IV.
• Provides broad Gram-negative coverage (E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus, Enterobacter). Can be stepped down to oral therapy (e.g., fluoroquinolone, co-trimoxazole) after culture results.
• Meropenem is reserved for multidrug-resistant/ESBL-producing organisms.
• Amoxicillin-clavulanate and Nitrofurantoin are for uncomplicated cystitis, not for systemic/upper tract infections.

Other Options:
Option B: Meropenem
• Very broad-spectrum; used if culture shows ESBL/CRE. Not first-line empiric.
Option C: Amoxicillin-clavulanate
• Limited role in complicated UTIs, not reliable empiric therapy.
Option D: Nitrofurantoin
• Only for uncomplicated cystitis; ineffective in pyelonephritis due to poor renal tissue penetration.

stem: A 58-year-old man presents with septic shock. He is febrile, tachycardic and has a blood pressure of 80/50 mmHg despite adequate fluid resuscitation with IV crystalloids. His urine output is low and he is drowsy. What is the next best step in management?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock.
• Acts mainly on alpha-1 receptors -> increased systemic vascular resistance -> restores blood pressure. Also stimulates beta-1 receptors -> supports cardiac output.
• Improves perfusion of vital organs (brain, kidney, heart).
• If MAP remains low despite norepinephrine, vasopressin or epinephrine can be added.
• Hydrocortisone is reserved for refractory shock unresponsive to fluids + vasopressors.

Other Options:
Option A: Dopamine
• Previously used, but now avoided due to increased arrhythmia risk and worse mortality vs norepinephrine.
Option C: Hydrocortisone
• Used in refractory septic shock (after norepinephrine + vasopressin), not immediately after fluids.
Option D: Vasopressin
• Added as second-line adjunct in norepinephrine-resistant shock.

stem: Which of the following is FALSE regarding Bempedoic acid?
options: Non Competitive Inhibitor ATP citrate lyase
Can be used with statins for hypercholesterolemia
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Bempedoic acid competitively inhibits ATP citrate lyase, reducing cholesterol biosynthesis.
• It is a dicarboxylic acid derivative prodrug, activated only in the liver (not in muscle -> less risk of myopathy).
• Can be safely combined with statins or used as an alternative in statin-intolerant patients.
• Thus, all other statements are true except 'non-competitive inhibitor'.

Other Options:
Option B: It inhibits cholesterol biosynthesis
• True; by inhibiting ATP citrate lyase upstream of HMG-CoA reductase.
Option C: Can be used with statins for hypercholesterolemia
• True; provides additive LDL-C lowering effect.
Option D: It is a dicarboxylic acid
• True; structurally a synthetic dicarboxylic acid derivative.

stem: A patient presented with perianal pruritus specially during night. Eggs as shown were seen on scotch tape examination. What is the likely organism?
subject: Microbiology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Classically causes nocturnal perianal itching due to gravid females migrating to perianal skin at night.
• Scotch/cellophane tape test picks up planoconvex, ovoid, translucent eggs -> diagnostic for pinworm.
• Eggs are deposited perianally (not in stool), hence stool microscopy is often negative. Transmission is fecal-oral via autoinfection; common in children and household contacts. Treatment: Albendazole/Mebendazole with repeat dose at 2 weeks and treatment of close contacts; emphasize hygiene and nail care.

Other Options:
Option A: Ancylostoma duodenale
• Hookworm; causes iron-deficiency anemia via blood loss.
• Eggs are thin-shelled, segmented in stool, not on scotch tape.
Option C: Hymenolepis nana
• Dwarf tapeworm; cestode with embryonated eggs in stool may cause autoinfection and abdominal symptoms.
• Does not present with classic nocturnal perianal pruritus.
Option D: Trichuris trichiura
• Whipworm; barrel-shaped eggs with bipolar plugs in stool; causes dysentery/rectal prolapse in heavy infections.
• Not diagnosed by scotch-tape test.

stem: Which HPV genotype is commonly implicated in cervical cancer?
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• HPV 16 and 18 are the most oncogenic genotypes, accounting for ~70% of cervical cancers.
• They are classified as high-risk HPV types, integrating viral DNA into host genome -> expression of E6 (p53 inactivation) and E7 (Rb inactivation) -> malignant transformation.
• Other high-risk types: 31, 33, 35, 45 (also associated with cervical and anogenital cancers, but less frequent).
• HPV 6 and 11 are low-risk types, causing benign lesions such as genital warts (condyloma acuminata) and respiratory papillomatosis.
• HPV 1 and 5 are associated with cutaneous warts, not cervical cancer.

Other Options:
Option A: 6, 11
• Low-risk HPV types; cause genital warts, not cervical cancer.
Option C: 1, 5
• Cause skin warts, not implicated in cervical malignancy.
Option D: 31, 33
• High-risk HPV types, associated with cervical cancer, but less common compared to 16 and 18.

stem: Autoinfection is seen in case of which parasitic infection?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Strongyloides stercoralis is the classical example of autoinfection.
• Normally, rhabditiform larvae pass in stool -> develop into infective filariform larvae in soil -> reinfect via skin.
• In autoinfection, rhabditiform larvae transform into filariform larvae inside the intestine itself.
• These penetrate the intestinal mucosa or perianal skin, enter circulation and restart the life cycle within the same host.
• Consequence: persistent infection for decades; in immunocompromised patients -> hyperinfection syndrome (disseminated strongyloidiasis, often fatal).

Other Options:
Option A: Ascaris lumbricoides
• No autoinfection. Eggs must embryonate in soil to become infective.
Option B: Ancylostoma duodenale (Hookworm)
• No autoinfection. Infective filariform larvae develop in soil and penetrate skin.
Option D: Hymenolepis nana
• Can also undergo autoinfection via its eggs in intestine, but in exams the single best answer to this question is Strongyloides stercoralis, because it is the most important and classical cause.

stem: Which of the following is not a thermally dimorphic fungus?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Thermally dimorphic fungi exist as mold (mycelial form) in environment at 25°C and as yeast (or yeast-like form) in human tissue at 37°C.
• Examples: Histoplasma capsulatum, Blastomyces dermatitidis, Coccidioides immitis, Paracoccidioides brasiliensis, Sporothrix schenckii.
• Cryptococcus neoformans is not dimorphic:
1. It exists only as yeast in both environment and tissue.
2. Has a characteristic polysaccharide capsule, visible on India ink staining.
3. Causes cryptococcal meningitis, especially in HIV/AIDS patients.

Other Options:
Option A: Coccidioides immitis
• Yes, dimorphic. In tissue, forms spherules with endospores.
Option B: Histoplasma capsulatum
• Yes, dimorphic. In tissue, appears as intracellular yeast within macrophages.
Option D: Blastomyces dermatitidis
• Yes, dimorphic. In tissue, appears as broad-based budding yeast.

stem:
Match the following:
Column AColumn B
A. Tick1. Rickettsial pox
B. Louse2. Endemic typhus
C. Mite3. Epidemic typhus
D. Rat flea4. RMSF
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Tick is the vector for Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (RMSF) caused by Rickettsia rickettsii (A-4).
• Louse (Human body louse) is the vector for Epidemic typhus caused by Rickettsia prowazekii (B-3).
• Mite (Liponyssoides sanguineus) is the vector for Rickettsial pox caused by Rickettsia akari (C-1).
• Rat flea (Xenopsylla cheopis) is the vector for Endemic typhus (Murine typhus) caused by Rickettsia typhi (D-2).
• Thus, the correct matching is A-4, B-3, C-1, D-2.

Other Options:
Option A: A-1, B-2, C-3, D-4
Incorrectly matches Tick with Rickettsial pox and Rat flea with RMSF.
Option C: A-3, B-1, C-2, D-4
Incorrectly matches Louse with Rickettsial pox and Tick with Epidemic typhus.
Option D: A-2, B-4, C-3, D-1
Incorrectly matches Tick with Endemic typhus and Louse with RMSF.

stem: The phenomenon of capsular swelling when mixed with specific anti-capsular antibodies is shown by which organism?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• This phenomenon is known as the Quellung reaction (capsular swelling test).
• When anti-capsular antibodies are mixed with Streptococcus pneumoniae, the capsule becomes swollen, highly refractile, and sharply defined under the microscope.
• This reaction confirms the presence of the capsule and is a classical, highly specific diagnostic feature used for typing strains of Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcus).

Other Options:
Option B: Haemophilus influenzae
Also an encapsulated organism (type b is highly virulent), but typically detected by latex agglutination or PCR in modern settings, not classically associated as the primary textbook example for the Quellung reaction compared to pneumococcus.
Option C: Clostridium tetani
Identified by its classic drumstick appearance (terminal spores) and lacks a capsule that undergoes swelling.
Option D: Corynebacterium diphtheriae
Produces volutin (metachromatic) granules seen on Albert's stain; it is non-capsulated and thus shows no Quellung reaction.

stem: An immunoglobulin is shown with a secretory component and a J chain. Which isotype is it?
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Secretory IgA (sIgA) is a dimer joined by a J (joining) chain and stabilized by a secretory component.
• The secretory component is derived from the polymeric Ig receptor on epithelial cells and protects the immunoglobulin from proteolytic degradation in harsh mucosal environments.
• It is the major immunoglobulin of mucosal immunity, predominantly found in saliva, tears, colostrum, breast milk, and respiratory and intestinal secretions.
• Plays a critical role in preventing pathogen adhesion and colonization on mucosal epithelial surfaces.

Other Options:
Option A: IgM
Exists as a pentamer linked by a J chain in the serum, but it does not possess a secretory component.
Option C: IgG
Exists as a monomer, lacking both a J chain and a secretory component. It is the major immunoglobulin in the serum.
Option D: IgD
Exists as a monomer, mostly found on the surface of immature B cells functioning as a B-cell receptor; no secretory component.

stem: Kanagawa phenomenon is shown by:
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The Kanagawa phenomenon is defined as the beta-hemolysis of human red blood cells on Wagatsuma agar.
• This phenomenon is exclusively caused by the production of a thermostable direct hemolysin (TDH).
• It is the hallmark characteristic of Vibrio parahaemolyticus, a halophilic marine bacterium responsible for causing acute gastroenteritis (especially following the ingestion of raw or undercooked seafood).
• This test effectively differentiates pathogenic strains (which are Kanagawa-positive) from non-pathogenic environmental strains.

Other Options:
Option A: Staphylococcus aureus
Causes typical beta-hemolysis on standard sheep blood agar due to various hemolysins, but not the specific Kanagawa phenomenon on Wagatsuma agar.
Option C: Vibrio cholerae
Causes severe cholera with profuse watery diarrhea but is generally non-hemolytic and does not produce TDH.
Option D: Shigella dysenteriae
Produces Shiga toxin leading to bacillary dysentery; it does not exhibit the Kanagawa phenomenon.

stem: Prozone phenomenon is seen in which of the following conditions?
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• The Prozone phenomenon is a classic false-negative reaction in serological agglutination or precipitation tests due to an overwhelmingly high antibody concentration in the patient's serum.
• This antibody excess prevents optimal cross-linking and lattice formation with the antigen.
• It is classically and most frequently observed in secondary syphilis testing (like the VDRL test), where the antibody titers peak to extremely high levels.
• The phenomenon is overcome and the true titer revealed by performing serial dilutions of the serum before testing.

Other Options:
Option A: Typhoid fever (Widal test)
The Widal test can show false positives (due to cross-reactivity or previous vaccination) or false negatives (if tested too early), but the prozone phenomenon is not its classic diagnostic pitfall.
Option C: Infectious mononucleosis (Paul-Bunnell test)
Detects heterophile antibodies; the prozone phenomenon is not characteristic here.
Option D: Leptospirosis (MAT)
MAT utilizes live antigens and is the gold standard, but the prozone phenomenon is not a typical hallmark associated with it.

stem: Which of the following statements is true?
options: Widal test is positive in the first week of enteric fever
Standard agglutination test is used for brucellosis
Live organisms are used in MAT
VDRL is a specific test for syphilis
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• The Standard Agglutination Test (SAT) is the most commonly used and reliable serological test for diagnosing brucellosis.
• It detects antibodies directed against the Brucella smooth lipopolysaccharide (LPS) antigens. It uses killed, standardized Brucella organisms as antigens, and titers >= 1:160 in endemic areas are considered diagnostically significant.

Other Options:
Option A: Widal test is positive in the first week of enteric fever
Incorrect because agglutinating antibodies typically appear only after 7-10 days (second week) of infection. Early first-week diagnosis relies primarily on blood culture.
Option C: Live organisms are used in MAT
Incorrect based on standard textbook explanations which note that handling live Leptospira is hazardous, hence many routine setups utilize killed/inactivated antigens (Note: While reference labs do use live antigens for the gold standard Microscopic Agglutination Test, in the context of this specific MCQ, Option B is the undisputed universally true statement).
Option D: VDRL is a specific test for syphilis
Incorrect. VDRL is a non-treponemal (non-specific) screening test that detects anti-cardiolipin (reagin) antibodies, which can yield biological false positives in conditions like SLE, leprosy, and pregnancy.

stem: Two morphological forms (trophozoite and cyst) are seen in all except:
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Trichomonas vaginalis exists exclusively as a trophozoite; it entirely lacks a cystic stage in its life cycle.
• Because it does not form a hardy, environmentally resistant cyst, it cannot survive well outside the host and is transmitted almost exclusively via direct sexual contact.
• Many other intestinal and free-living protozoa typically possess two forms: 1. Trophozoite (the active, vegetative, feeding, and multiplying form) and 2. Cyst (the dormant, infective, and environmentally resistant form).

Other Options:
Option A: Naegleria
Naegleria fowleri is a free-living ameba that exists in three forms: trophozoite, flagellate, and an environmentally resistant cyst.
Option C: Giardia
Giardia lamblia exhibits both the active trophozoite form (which attaches to the intestinal mucosa) and the infective cyst form (passed in feces).
Option D: Acanthamoeba
Acanthamoeba species possess both an active trophozoite stage and a highly resilient, double-walled cyst stage.

stem: Which of the following is not true regarding Toxoplasma gondii infection?
options: Tachyzoites are seen in acute infection
Bradyzoites are present in tissue cysts
Congenital infection is more severe if the mother acquires primary infection during pregnancy
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Freshly passed unsporulated oocysts in cat feces are not immediately infective to humans or other intermediate hosts.
• They must undergo sporulation in the external environment, which typically requires 1 to 5 days under favorable conditions (appropriate temperature and humidity), to become mature, infective sporulated oocysts.
• Therefore, the statement 'Freshly passed oocyst is infective' is false.

Other Options:
Option B: Tachyzoites are seen in acute infection
True; tachyzoites are the rapidly multiplying, invasive forms found in the blood and tissues during the active, acute phase of infection.
Option C: Bradyzoites are present in tissue cysts
True; bradyzoites are the slowly dividing, dormant forms sequestered inside tissue cysts (especially in the brain and skeletal muscle), responsible for chronic, latent infection.
Option D: Congenital infection is more severe if the mother acquires primary infection during pregnancy
True; a primary maternal infection during pregnancy poses a severe risk of transplacental transmission, potentially causing devastating fetal anomalies, unlike a pre-existing latent infection.

stem: An HIV positive patient presented with white patches in the oral cavity. What is the likely microscopic appearance of sample from such patches?
options: Budding yeast like cells and pseudohyphae
Septate hyphae branching at 45 degree angle
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• In HIV-positive and other immunocompromised patients, the presence of adherent white patches (thrush) in the oral cavity is most often due to oropharyngeal candidiasis caused by Candida albicans.
• Microscopic examination of scrapings from these patches (prepared with a KOH mount, Gram stain, or PAS stain) classically reveals:
1. Spherical to oval budding yeast-like cells (blastoconidia).
2. Elongated, sausage-like formations called pseudohyphae.
• This combination of budding yeasts and pseudohyphae is pathognomonic for tissue invasion by Candida species.

Other Options:
Option B: Septate hyphae branching at 45 degree angle
Describes the microscopic morphology of Aspergillus species, which cause invasive pulmonary aspergillosis, not typical oral white patches.
Option C: MNGs with intranuclear inclusions
Multinucleated giant cells (MNGs) with Cowdry Type A intranuclear inclusions are seen in Herpes simplex virus (HSV) infections (detectable via Tzanck smear), not candidiasis.
Option D: Acid-fast bacilli
Seen in Mycobacterium tuberculosis or MAC infections (highlighted by Ziehl-Neelsen stain), which do not present as easily scraped oral white patches.

stem: Elek's gel precipitation test is used for the demonstration of which of the following?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Elek's gel precipitation test is an established in vitro immunodiffusion assay used specifically to demonstrate the toxigenicity (exotoxin production) of Corynebacterium diphtheriae isolates.
• Procedure: A strip of filter paper impregnated with diphtheria antitoxin is placed on the surface of an agar plate. The test organisms are then streaked at right angles to the filter paper strip.
• If the bacterial strain is toxigenic, it secretes the toxin, which diffuses through the agar. Where the diffusing toxin meets the diffusing antitoxin at optimal proportions, visible white lines of precipitation form at 45-degree angles, confirming toxin production.

Other Options:
Option A: Capsular swelling in Streptococcus pneumoniae
Demonstrated by the Quellung reaction using specific antisera, not Elek's test.
Option C: Detection of antibodies in syphilis
Performed using serological tests such as VDRL, RPR, FTA-ABS, or TPHA, not Elek's test.
Option D: Identification of Clostridium tetani toxin
Confirmed by in vivo mouse inoculation neutralization tests, not by in vitro Elek's precipitation.

stem: Which of the following features are associated with granulosa cell tumors, a type of sex cord-stromal tumor of the ovary?
subject: Pathology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Call-Exner bodies are the classic, hallmark microscopic feature of granulosa cell tumors.
• These structures appear as small, follicle-like, gland-like spaces filled with acidophilic (eosinophilic) fluid and cellular debris, closely resembling immature primordial follicles.
• Granulosa cell tumors are sex cord-stromal tumors of the ovary that typically secrete estrogen (leading to endometrial hyperplasia or precocious puberty) and exhibit inhibin positivity on immunohistochemistry.
• However, histologically, the most pathognomonic diagnostic finding is the presence of Call-Exner bodies.

Other Options:
Option B: Inhibin positivity
While this is a true immunohistochemical marker for granulosa cell tumors, Call-Exner bodies represent the defining, most frequently tested morphological feature.
Option C: Sex cord tumor origin
This is simply the broad classification category, not a specific diagnostic feature.
Option D: CA-125 elevation
False; CA-125 is a serum tumor marker classically elevated in epithelial ovarian cancers (like serous cystadenocarcinoma), not in sex cord-stromal tumors.

stem: A woman presents with bilateral, multilobulated ovarian cysts containing solid components. Imaging reveals the presence of ascites and omental caking. Her serum CA-125 level is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Serous carcinoma is the most common malignant epithelial tumor of the ovary, and it frequently presents bilaterally.
• Radiological features: Complex, multiloculated cystic masses with significant solid components, papillary projections, and thick septations.
• It is highly aggressive and commonly presents at an advanced stage with widespread peritoneal dissemination, leading to massive ascites and 'omental caking' (thickened, nodular omentum).
• Serum CA-125: Markedly elevated, serving as an important diagnostic and monitoring tumor marker for epithelial ovarian cancers.
• Histology: Characterized by complex papillary architecture, marked cellular atypia, and concentric calcifications known as psammoma bodies.

Other Options:
Option A: Granulosa cell tumor
A sex cord-stromal tumor that is usually unilateral, produces estrogen (causing abnormal bleeding), and is not typically associated with massive ascites, omental caking, or high CA-125.
Option B: Mucinous cystadenoma
A benign epithelial tumor that can grow to massive sizes but is usually unilateral, filled with thick mucin, and lacks the aggressive peritoneal spread (omental caking) seen here.
Option D: Endometrioma
Also known as a 'chocolate cyst,' it is a localized manifestation of endometriosis. It may cause pelvic pain and mild CA-125 elevation but does not cause malignant omental caking.

stem: A 26-year-old man presents with an 8-month history of chronic diarrhea. Barium enema reveals a rigid ("lead-pipe") colon with loss of haustral markings.
subject: Medicine, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
• Ulcerative colitis is an idiopathic chronic inflammatory bowel disease that specifically involves the mucosa and submucosa of the colon and rectum.
• Classic radiological sign: The 'Lead-pipe' appearance on barium enema. This occurs because chronic, recurrent inflammation and healing lead to mucosal atrophy, muscular hypertrophy, and fibrosis, causing a complete loss of the normal colonic haustrations, resulting in a smooth, rigid, tubular structure.
• Clinical features: Patients typically present with chronic, relapsing episodes of bloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, tenesmus, and urgency.
• Pathology: Features continuous, unbroken mucosal inflammation that invariably begins at the rectum and extends proximally to a variable extent.

Other Options:
Option A: Pseudomembranous colitis
An acute infectious colitis caused by Clostridioides difficile toxins, usually following broad-spectrum antibiotic use. Endoscopy shows raised yellowish plaques (pseudomembranes), not a chronic lead-pipe colon.
Option C: Crohn's disease
A transmural inflammatory bowel disease that can affect any part of the GIT (mouth to anus) with skip lesions. Radiology typically shows a 'string sign' (narrowing of the terminal ileum due to strictures), deep fissuring ulcers, and a cobblestone appearance, rather than a continuous lead-pipe colon.
Option D: Intestinal tuberculosis
Usually involves the ileocecal region. Radiological findings include strictures, a deformed, pulled-up cecum, and the 'Fleischner sign' (widened gap between the ileum and cecum).

stem: A patient presents with multiple punched-out lytic lesions in the skull. Which of the following thyroid malignancies is most likely associated with this radiological finding?
subject: Pathology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Follicular carcinoma of the thyroid is unique among well-differentiated thyroid cancers because it characteristically spreads early via the hematogenous route (bloodstream).
• In contrast, papillary carcinoma preferentially spreads via local lymphatics to regional cervical lymph nodes.
• Due to this hematogenous spread, follicular carcinoma commonly metastasizes to distant organs, particularly the bones, lungs, and liver.
• In the skeletal system, it frequently produces highly vascular, osteolytic 'punched-out' bone lesions, with the skull and long bones being classic sites. This clinical feature makes follicular carcinoma the thyroid malignancy most distinctly linked with lytic bone metastases.

Other Options:
Option A: Papillary carcinoma of thyroid
The most common thyroid malignancy, but it predominantly spreads via lymphatics to cervical nodes. Distant bony lytic metastases are rare and late occurrences.
Option B: Thyroid lymphoma
A rare malignancy, strongly associated with pre-existing Hashimoto's thyroiditis. It presents as a rapidly enlarging, compressive neck mass rather than with isolated bony metastases.
Option D: Medullary carcinoma of thyroid
A neuroendocrine tumor arising from the parafollicular C-cells. It secretes calcitonin and can metastasize to both nodes and viscera, but it is not classically characterized by multiple punched-out lytic skull lesions.

stem: Which of the following is NOT an aneuploidy?
subject: Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Aneuploidy is a cytogenetic abnormality defined as the presence of an abnormal number of chromosomes in a cell (e.g., having 45 or 47 chromosomes instead of the usual 46), resulting from the gain or loss of individual chromosomes, but not a complete extra set.
• Bloom syndrome is NOT an aneuploidy. It is a rare autosomal recessive genetic disorder caused by mutations in the BLM gene, which encodes a RecQ DNA helicase.
• This defect leads to severe genomic instability, excessive homologous recombination (high rate of sister chromatid exchanges), growth deficiency, sun-sensitive facial erythema, and a high predisposition to early-onset cancers. The chromosome number itself remains a normal 46.

Other Options:
Option B: Klinefelter syndrome
This is a sex chromosome aneuploidy, characterized by the karyotype 47,XXY (an extra X chromosome in males).
Option C: Trisomy 13
Also known as Patau syndrome, this is an autosomal aneuploidy characterized by an extra copy of chromosome 13 (47,XX,+13 or 47,XY,+13).
Option D: Trisomy 21
Also known as Down syndrome, this is the most common autosomal aneuploidy, resulting from an extra copy of chromosome 21.

stem: Which of the following cancers is NOT associated with obesity?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Obesity is a well-established, major risk factor for several types of malignancies. Adipose tissue is metabolically active, driving carcinogenesis through mechanisms such as chronic low-grade inflammation, hyperinsulinemia (which increases IGF-1 activity), and the peripheral conversion of androgens to elevated levels of bioavailable estrogens.
• Cancers strongly linked to obesity include endometrial, breast (postmenopausal), colorectal, renal cell, esophageal, pancreatic, multiple myeloma, and meningioma (especially in females).
• Stomach (gastric) cancer is NOT classically categorized as an obesity-driven malignancy. Its primary, well-documented risk factors include Helicobacter pylori infection, chronic atrophic gastritis, high dietary intake of salted/smoked foods and nitrosamines, tobacco smoking, pernicious anemia, and a history of partial gastrectomy.

Other Options:
Option A: Meningioma
Epidemiological studies have shown a consistent positive association between increased BMI and the risk of meningioma, likely mediated by elevated estrogen exposure.
Option B: Pancreatic cancer
The risk of pancreatic cancer is significantly increased by obesity and its associated metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes.
Option D: Multiple myeloma
Multiple myeloma incidence is higher in overweight and obese individuals, potentially due to the chronic inflammatory state (e.g., elevated IL-6) promoted by adiposity.

stem: What is the most common inherited bleeding disorder?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Von Willebrand disease (vWD) is universally recognized as the most common inherited bleeding disorder, with an estimated global prevalence of approximately 1% of the general population.
• It is typically inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern (most commonly Type 1) and is caused by a quantitative deficiency or qualitative dysfunction of von Willebrand factor (vWF).
• vWF serves two critical hemostatic roles: 1. It acts as the essential bridging molecule mediating platelet adhesion to exposed subendothelial collagen at sites of vascular injury. 2. It acts as a protective carrier protein for circulating Factor VIII, preventing its rapid proteolytic degradation.
• Clinical features predominantly involve mucocutaneous bleeding: easy bruising, prolonged bleeding from minor cuts, excessive bleeding after dental extractions, severe menorrhagia, and recurrent epistaxis.

Other Options:
Option A: Hemophilia A
An X-linked recessive disorder caused by a deficiency of Factor VIII. It primarily affects males and causes deep tissue and joint bleeding (hemarthrosis), but it is much less common than vWD.
Option B: Hemophilia B
Also known as Christmas disease, an X-linked recessive disorder caused by Factor IX deficiency. It has a clinical presentation identical to Hemophilia A but is even rarer.
Option D: Factor V deficiency
Also known as Owren's disease or parahemophilia, this is an extremely rare autosomal recessive coagulation disorder.

stem: Caspase-1 is involved in which of the following processes?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Caspase-1 is the central enzymatic mediator of pyroptosis, a highly inflammatory form of programmed lytic cell death.
• Pyroptosis occurs primarily in macrophages and dendritic cells in response to intracellular infections, damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), and the assembly of the inflammasome complex (e.g., NLRP3 inflammasome).
• Upon activation within the inflammasome, Caspase-1 performs two crucial functions:
1. It cleaves the inactive precursors pro-IL-1beta and pro-IL-18 into their biologically active, highly proinflammatory forms (IL-1beta and IL-18).
2. It cleaves the protein gasdermin D. The N-terminal fragment of gasdermin D then oligomerizes in the cell membrane to form large pores, inducing rapid cell swelling, lysis, and the explosive release of inflammatory cytokines.

Other Options:
Option A: Apoptosis
A non-inflammatory, immunologically silent form of programmed cell death mediated mainly by the executioner caspases (Caspases 3, 6, and 7) and initiator caspases (Caspases 8, 9, and 10). Caspase-1 is not involved in apoptosis.
Option B: Necroptosis
A regulated form of necrosis that is completely caspase-independent. It is mediated by the kinases RIPK1 and RIPK3, which phosphorylate and activate the effector protein MLKL to form membrane pores.
Option C: Ferroptosis
An iron-dependent form of non-apoptotic cell death characterized by the toxic accumulation of lipid reactive oxygen species (lipid peroxides) when glutathione-dependent antioxidant defenses fail. It involves no caspases.

stem: Erythropoietin is useful in the treatment of which of the following types of anemia?
options: Anemia due to chronic kidney disease (CKD)
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Erythropoietin (EPO) is an essential glycoprotein hormone primarily synthesized and secreted by the peritubular interstitial fibroblasts of the renal cortex in response to tissue hypoxia.
• It acts on erythroid progenitor cells in the bone marrow to stimulate their survival, proliferation, and differentiation into mature red blood cells.
• In Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD), the progressive destruction of renal parenchyma leads to a profound deficiency of endogenous erythropoietin, resulting in a normocytic, normochromic anemia.
• The administration of exogenous recombinant human erythropoietin (e.g., epoetin alfa, darbepoetin alfa) is highly effective and represents the standard of care for treating the specific anemia associated with CKD, successfully raising hemoglobin levels and reducing the need for blood transfusions.

Other Options:
Option A: Nutritional anemia
Anemias caused by deficiencies of vital building blocks (iron, folic acid, or vitamin B12). The bone marrow has adequate EPO but lacks raw materials. Treatment requires specific nutrient replacement, not exogenous EPO.
Option C: Myelofibrotic disorders
Conditions characterized by extensive fibrotic replacement of the bone marrow space, leading to bone marrow failure. The stem cells cannot physically respond to EPO stimulation.
Option D: Aplastic anemia
A life-threatening condition defined by profound pancytopenia due to immune-mediated destruction or primary failure of hematopoietic stem cells. Endogenous EPO levels are usually already sky-high; exogenous EPO is useless because there are no viable stem cells to respond.

stem: Which of the following statements is FALSE regarding gene-cancer associations?
subject: Pathology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• TP53 is a paramount tumor suppressor gene ('the guardian of the genome'). Germline mutations in TP53 are responsible for Li-Fraumeni syndrome, a highly penetrant autosomal dominant disorder that dramatically predisposes individuals to a wide spectrum of early-onset malignancies, most notably breast cancer, bone and soft-tissue sarcomas, brain tumors, leukemias, and adrenocortical carcinoma.
• Mucosal neuromas (painless, bump-like neural tumors on the lips, tongue, and gastrointestinal tract) are the pathognomonic phenotypic feature of Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2B (MEN 2B) syndrome.
• MEN 2B is caused by specific activating mutations in the RET proto-oncogene, NOT by mutations in the TP53 gene. Therefore, the association 'TP53-Mucosal neuroma' is fundamentally false.

Other Options:
Option A: STK11-Carcinoma breast
Germline mutations in the STK11 (LKB1) gene cause Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, characterized by hamartomatous GI polyps and mucocutaneous pigmentation, and it carries a significantly increased risk for several malignancies, including breast, pancreatic, and gastrointestinal carcinomas.
Option B: BRCA2-Prostate cancer
Mutations in the BRCA2 tumor suppressor gene, classically known for hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome, also confer a significantly elevated risk for developing aggressive prostate cancer in males, as well as male breast cancer and pancreatic cancer.
Option D: PTEN-Thyroid lesions
Germline mutations in the PTEN tumor suppressor gene cause Cowden syndrome (PTEN Hamartoma Tumor Syndrome). This syndrome is heavily characterized by benign and malignant thyroid lesions (especially follicular thyroid cancer), as well as an increased risk for breast and endometrial carcinomas.

stem: Which of the following renal histological features is characteristically seen in diabetic nephropathy but not typically seen in other glomerulonephritides?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Diabetic nephropathy is the leading cause of end-stage renal disease worldwide. A hallmark of its advanced stage is nodular glomerulosclerosis, characterized by the formation of Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules.
• These nodules appear as prominent, spherical, eosinophilic, PAS-positive, laminated masses of acellular hyaline matrix situated peripherally within the mesangial core of the glomerulus.
• While diffuse mesangial sclerosis and GBM thickening are also present, the Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules are virtually pathognomonic for diabetic nephropathy and are not a feature of other primary or secondary glomerulonephritides.

Other Options:
Option B: Mesangial sclerosis
Diffuse mesangial sclerosis (an increase in the mesangial matrix) is a prominent and early feature of diabetic nephropathy, but it is entirely non-specific. It is observed in numerous other glomerular diseases, such as IgA nephropathy and membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis.
Option C: GBM thickening
Capillary basement membrane thickening is one of the earliest ultrastructural changes in diabetes. However, it is also the defining feature of membranous nephropathy and occurs in many chronic glomerulonephritides.
Option D: Foot process effacement
The loss or flattening of podocyte foot processes is the universal morphological correlate of proteinuria. It is the defining diagnostic feature of Minimal Change Disease and Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), but it is a non-specific finding in any heavily proteinuric state, including diabetes.

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with a breast lump. Biopsy reveals DCIS with comedo necrosis. Her mother died of breast cancer at 31. Due to financial constraints, only one genetic mutation test can be performed. Which of the following genes should be tested first?
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• The BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes are the most critical, high-penetrance tumor suppressor genes implicated in Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer (HBOC) syndrome.
• A mutation in BRCA1 confers a lifetime risk of breast cancer of up to 70-80%, and these cancers tend to present at an unusually young age.
• BRCA1-associated breast cancers characteristically exhibit aggressive histopathological features: they are frequently high-grade, 'triple-negative' (estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and HER2/neu negative), and have a high propensity to present as extensive high-grade Ductal Carcinoma In Situ (DCIS) with prominent comedo-type necrosis or as invasive ductal carcinomas.
