• The patient's remarkably young age of onset (24 years) coupled with her mother's tragic early death from breast cancer at 31 creates an overwhelmingly strong clinical suspicion for a BRCA1 mutation. Therefore, BRCA1 is the absolute priority for initial genetic screening.

Other Options:
Option B: PTEN
Mutations in PTEN cause Cowden syndrome. While it increases breast cancer risk, it is classically associated with mucocutaneous hamartomas (trichilemmomas), macrocephaly, and thyroid/endometrial cancers, lacking the specific aggressive triple-negative/comedo phenotype typical of BRCA1.
Option C: TP53
Germline mutations in TP53 cause Li-Fraumeni syndrome, predisposing to early-onset breast cancer. While it is a differential consideration in very young breast cancer patients, BRCA mutations are statistically much more common in familial breast cancer clusters and are the standard first-tier test.
Option D: CDH
Mutations in the CDH1 gene (encoding E-cadherin) are responsible for Hereditary Diffuse Gastric Cancer syndrome, which uniquely predisposes females to invasive lobular carcinoma of the breast, not the ductal carcinoma with comedo necrosis described in this vignette.

stem: In which of the following immunodeficiency disorders is HLA mismatched transplant rejection least likely, due to absent or severely impaired immune response?
subject: Pathology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Bare lymphocyte syndrome (BLS) is an exceedingly rare, severe primary immunodeficiency disorder defined by the defective or completely absent expression of Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) molecules on the surface of cells.
• Type I BLS involves a lack of MHC Class I molecules, while the more severe Type II BLS involves a profound lack of MHC Class II molecules due to mutations in transcription factors governing their expression.
• Because CD4+ helper T cells rely entirely on MHC Class II presentation for their activation and maturation, patients with Type II BLS suffer from a catastrophic failure of CD4+ T cell function, leading to a Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID)-like clinical picture.
• Transplant rejection, especially acute cellular rejection, is a process fundamentally driven by recipient T cells recognizing foreign (mismatched) HLA antigens. In BLS, the patient's adaptive immune system is so profoundly crippled that it is incapable of mounting this alloreactive T-cell response. Consequently, an HLA-mismatched graft is least likely to be rejected by a patient with BLS.

Other Options:
Option A: Chediak-Higashi syndrome
A lysosomal trafficking defect primarily affecting phagocytic function (neutrophils and macrophages) and causing partial albinism. The adaptive T-cell immune system remains sufficiently intact to mount a vigorous allograft rejection response.
Option B: Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome
An X-linked disorder characterized by eczema, thrombocytopenia, and combined immunodeficiency due to cytoskeletal defects. Although T cell function declines over time, these patients still possess the immunological machinery capable of graft rejection.
Option D: Job syndrome
Also known as Hyper-IgE syndrome, caused by STAT3 mutations. It leads to defective Th17 differentiation and impaired neutrophil recruitment, presenting with cold staphylococcal abscesses and extreme IgE elevation, but T-cell alloreactivity remains intact.

stem: Helicobacter pylori is implicated in the pathogenesis of peptic ulcer disease. Why do only some infected individuals develop ulcers?
options: All individuals infected with H. pylori will eventually develop ulcers
Ulcer development depends on the interaction between host factors and bacterial virulence
H. pylori is only pathogenic when associated with antibiotic resistance
Usually self limiting, needs no treatment
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Helicobacter pylori infection is extraordinarily prevalent, colonizing the gastric mucosa of over 50% of the global population. Despite this high prevalence, the vast majority of infected individuals experience only asymptomatic, mild chronic gastritis.
• Only a specific subset of infected individuals (roughly 10-15%) progresses to develop clinically significant disease, such as peptic ulcer disease (gastric or duodenal ulcers), and an even smaller fraction (1-3%) develops gastric adenocarcinoma or MALT lymphoma.
• This variable clinical outcome is dictated by a complex, dynamic interplay of three main factors:
1. Bacterial virulence factors: Strains expressing potent cytotoxins like CagA (cytotoxin-associated gene A) and VacA (vacuolating cytotoxin A) provoke a much more aggressive and destructive inflammatory response.
2. Host genetic and immunological factors: Polymorphisms in host cytokine genes (such as those leading to high production of pro-inflammatory IL-1beta) significantly increase susceptibility to severe mucosal damage and hypochlorhydria.
3. Environmental influences: Concurrent use of NSAIDs, chronic smoking, psychological stress, and high-salt diets further compromise the gastric mucosal barrier, acting synergistically with the bacteria to promote ulceration.

Other Options:
Option A: All individuals infected with H. pylori will eventually develop ulcers
False; the vast majority of infected individuals remain entirely asymptomatic throughout their lives.
Option C: H. pylori is only pathogenic when associated with antibiotic resistance
False; antibiotic resistance makes the organism harder to eradicate clinically, but it does not inherently alter its primary pathogenic capability to cause mucosal damage.
Option D: Usually self limiting, needs no treatment
False; H. pylori infection almost always results in a chronic, lifelong infection unless deliberately eradicated with specific combination antibiotic therapy.

stem: Which of the following stains is not used for the identification of fungi?
subject: Pathology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Congo red is a specialized histochemical stain universally utilized in pathology specifically for the detection and definitive diagnosis of amyloidosis, not fungal elements.
• When amyloid deposits in tissues are stained with Congo red, they appear salmon-pink under normal light microscopy. When viewed under cross-polarized light, these stained deposits exhibit a highly characteristic and diagnostic 'apple-green' birefringence due to the unique beta-pleated sheet conformation of the amyloid fibrils.
• Congo red has no utility in visualizing the carbohydrate-rich cell walls or specific structural components of fungi.

Other Options:
Option B: Gomori methenamine silver (GMS)
A premier, highly sensitive fungal stain. It relies on the oxidation of carbohydrates in the fungal cell wall to aldehydes, which then reduce silver nitrate to visible metallic silver, rendering fungal hyphae and yeasts sharply black against a pale green background.
Option C: Fontana-Masson
A specialized argentaffin stain primarily used to identify melanin. In mycology, it is crucial for demonstrating the presence of melanin in the cell walls of dematiaceous (naturally brown/black) fungi and Cryptococcus neoformans.
Option D: Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS)
A very common histological stain that oxidizes complex carbohydrates (glycogen and mucopolysaccharides) abundant in the fungal cell wall, staining the fungal elements a vivid magenta/red color, contrasting sharply with background tissues.

stem: What is shown in the below image?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Respiratory bronchiole is the first part of the airway where gas exchange occurs.
• Histological features:
1. Lined by simple cuboidal epithelium with scattered cilia.
2. Presence of alveoli budding from their walls.
3. Transition zone between conducting and respiratory portions of the lung.
• Terminal bronchioles have cuboidal epithelium but no alveoli in their walls.

Other Options:
Option A: Trachea
Trachea contains C-shaped hyaline cartilage and respiratory epithelium.
Option B: Bronchus
Bronchus shows cartilage plates and submucosal glands.
Option D: Terminal bronchiole
Terminal bronchiole is lined by cuboidal epithelium, but has no alveolar openings.

stem: Which of the following HPV serotypes are associated with cervical carcinoma?
subject: Pathology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• High-risk HPV types are strongly associated with cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN) and invasive cervical carcinoma.
• The most important are HPV 16 and 18, together accounting for ~70% of cervical cancers.
• Other high-risk types include 31, 33, 35, 45, and 52.
• Low-risk types (6 and 11) cause benign lesions such as condyloma acuminatum and laryngeal papillomas, but not carcinoma.

Other Options:
Option A: HPV 6/11
HPV 6/11 are low-risk; cause genital warts, not cervical cancer.
Option C: HPV 31/33
HPV 31/33 are high-risk but less common than 16/18.
Option D: HPV 45/45
HPV 45 is high-risk, but not the most common cause compared to 16/18.

stem: In a hospital, patients are classified into three groups based on disease severity: mild, moderate, and severe. One patient is randomly selected from each group for a study. What type of sampling method is this?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Stratified random sampling involves dividing the study population into subgroups (strata) based on a characteristic (here, severity: mild, moderate, severe).
• From each stratum, a random sample is chosen -> ensuring representation of all subgroups.
• This increases precision and representativeness of the sample.

Other Options:
Option A: Simple random sampling
Simple random sampling involves every individual having an equal chance of being selected, without subgrouping.
Option B: Systematic sampling
Systematic sampling is selecting every kth individual from a list.
Option D: Cluster sampling
Cluster sampling involves randomly selecting whole clusters (e.g., villages, wards) and studying all members.

stem: During the Maha Kumbh Mela, mobile health units and medical bases were set up, along with multiple surveillance units for monitoring cases of fever, diarrhea, and respiratory illnesses. What was the primary purpose of setting up these surveillance units?
options: To reduce the need for medical professionals
For early diagnosis and detection of potential outbreak warning signs
For Immediate quarantine of all symptomatic Individuals
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Surveillance units are established in mass gatherings (like Kumbh Mela, Olympics, Hajj) to perform public health surveillance.
• Primary purpose: early detection of unusual disease trends (e.g., sudden increase in fever, diarrhea, respiratory illness) to identify potential outbreaks.
• This allows for timely preventive and control measures, including outbreak investigation, isolation if needed, and rapid treatment.

Other Options:
Option A: To reduce the need for medical professionals
False; surveillance does not replace healthcare staff, it supports them.
Option C: For Immediate quarantine of all symptomatic Individuals
Not all symptomatic individuals require immediate quarantine; surveillance focuses on detection and monitoring.
Option D: To provide curative treatment only
False; treatment is secondary. Surveillance is preventive and predictive.

stem: Arranging Research study sequence:
options: Formulating the research question -> Stating objectives -> Selecting study population -> Data analysis
Selecting study population -> Formulating research question -> Stating objectives -> Data analysis
Stating objectives -> Formulating research question -> Data analysis -> Selecting population
Data analysis -> Selecting population -> Stating objectives -> Formulating research question
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Step 1: Formulating the research question - Identify the problem and frame a clear, answerable research question.
• Step 2: Stating objectives - Translate the research question into specific, measurable objectives.
• Step 3: Selecting study population - Choose the target population/sample and appropriate study design.
• Step 4: Data analysis - After data collection, apply statistical tools to test hypotheses and interpret results.

Other Options:
Option B: Selecting study population -> Formulating research question -> Stating objectives -> Data analysis
Incorrect, because population cannot be selected before the research problem is defined.
Option C: Stating objectives -> Formulating research question -> Data analysis -> Selecting population
Incorrect, as objectives cannot precede the research question.
Option D: Data analysis -> Selecting population -> Stating objectives -> Formulating research question
Incorrect, since analysis is the final step, not the first.

stem: Quartiles and tertiles are examples of which type of statistical measure?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Quartiles and tertiles are positional measures (quantiles) that divide a dataset into equal parts.
• Quartiles divide data into four equal parts (Q1, Q2, Q3). Tertiles divide data into three equal parts.
• They indicate the relative position of observations within an ordered dataset, not the spread or central value.

Other Options:
Option A: Measure of central tendency
Measures of central tendency represent the central value of a dataset (Mean, Median, Mode).
Option B: Measure of dispersion
Measures of dispersion describe the spread or variability (Range, Variance, Standard deviation).
Option D: Measure of correlation
Measures of correlation assess relationships between two variables.

stem:
In a given population:
The number of Individuals aged 0-14 years = 6,000
The number of individuals aged 15-59 years = 12,000
The number of Individuals aged 60 years and above = 2,000.
What is the dependency ratio of this population?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Dependency ratio = ((Population aged 0-14 + Population aged >= 60) / Population aged 15-59) x 100.
• Calculation: ((6000 + 2000) / 12000) x 100 = (8000 / 12000) x 100 = 66.7%.
• This means there are 66.7 dependents for every 100 working-age individuals in the population.

Other Options:
Option B: 75%
75% is an overestimation.
Option C: 50%
50% is an underestimation.
Option D: 80%
80% is an overestimation.

stem: What is the minimum legal age at which a woman can consent for Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP) in India?
subject: FMT, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• According to the MTP Act, 1971 (amended 2021):
1. A woman must be 18 years or older to give valid consent for MTP.
2. If below 18 years, consent of a guardian is mandatory.
3. Married or unmarried status does not affect the minimum age requirement.
• Thus, the legal age for independent consent is 18 years.

Other Options:
Option A: 16 years
16 years is not valid under the Act. Consent requires a guardian if <18.
Option C: 20 years
20 years has no legal relevance for minimum age.
Option D: 24 years
24 years refers to the gestational age limit for MTP under the amended Act, not the woman's age.

stem: Which HIV latest criteria is not included in high-risk group?
options: Men who have sex with men
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• High-risk groups (HRGs) as per NACO, India include:
1. Female sex workers (FSWs)
2. Injecting drug users (IDUs)
3. Men who have sex with men (MSM)
4. Transgender/Hijra populations.
• These groups are considered core transmitters due to high-risk behaviors.
• Long distance truck drivers are considered a bridge population (they connect HRGs with the general population), but they are not categorized as HRG in the latest guidelines.

Other Options:
Option A: Sex worker
Sex workers are a Core HRG.
Option B: IV abuser's
IV drug abusers are a Core HRG.
Option D: Men who have sex with men
MSM are a Core HRG.

stem: The term "Gene pool" refers to which of the following?
options: The total number of genes present in an individual
The total number of alleles present in a population
The number of dominant genes in a species
The number of chromosomes in an organism
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Gene pool is defined as the complete set of genetic information (all alleles of all genes) in all members of a particular population.
• It reflects the genetic diversity of that population.
• A large gene pool = more genetic variation -> better adaptability to environmental changes.

Other Options:
Option A: The total number of genes present in an individual
The total number of genes in an individual describes the genome, not the gene pool.
Option C: The number of dominant genes in a species
Dominance/recessiveness does not define the gene pool.
Option D: The number of chromosomes in an organism
The number of chromosomes in an organism refers to its karyotype.

stem: According to the National Programme for Control of Blindness (NPCB) and WHO definition, blindness is defined as?
options: Visual acuity less than 3/60 after correction in better eye
Visual acuity less than 6/60 after correction in better eye
Visual acuity less than 3/60 without correction in better eye
Visual acuity less than 6/18 after correction in better eye
subject: PSM, Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Blindness (NPCB/WHO definition):
1. Visual acuity <3/60 in the better eye with best possible correction, OR
2. Visual field less than 10° around central fixation.
• This means the person can only see at 3 meters what a normal person can see at 60 meters. It indicates severe visual disability affecting independence and quality of life.

Other Options:
Option B: Visual acuity less than 6/60 after correction in better eye
<6/60 after correction defines severe visual impairment, sometimes grouped under low vision depending on the exact classification system.
Option C: Visual acuity less than 3/60 without correction in better eye
<3/60 without correction is incorrect, since the definition requires best corrected vision.
Option D: Visual acuity less than 6/18 after correction in better eye
<6/18 after correction defines visual impairment (low vision), not blindness.

stem: The RMNCH+A strategy by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India, aims to provide comprehensive healthcare across various life stages. Which of the following population groups is NOT a target of the RMNCH+A strategy?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• RMNCH+A stands for:
1. Reproductive health (family planning, maternal health)
2. Maternal health (antenatal, intranatal, postnatal care)
3. Newborn health (essential newborn care, immunization)
4. Child health (nutrition, diarrhea, pneumonia, ARI management)
5. Adolescent health.
• The strategy focuses on a continuum of care from adolescence to childbirth to childhood.
• Geriatric care is not covered under RMNCH+A; it falls under separate programs like the National Programme for Health Care of the Elderly (NPHCE).

Other Options:
Option A: Infant population
Infant population is targeted (newborn/infant care & immunization).
Option B: Family planning services
Family planning services are a core component under reproductive health.
Option D: Adolescent population
Adolescent population is targeted via the Adolescent Reproductive and Sexual Health (ARSH) strategy.

stem: Which of the following Acts is primarily concerned with the protection of personal medical data and privacy in India?
subject: PSM, FMT
explanation:
Solution
• The Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023 is the first comprehensive Indian law dealing with personal data protection, including health and medical data.
• It emphasizes:
1. Consent-based data sharing.
2. Right to privacy of personal medical records.
3. Data security obligations for hospitals, insurers, and digital health platforms.

Other Options:
Option A: PNDT Act
PNDT Act regulates use of prenatal diagnostics to prevent sex selection, not data privacy.
Option C: HIPAA
HIPAA is a US law for data privacy, not applicable in India.
Option D: MTP Act
MTP Act governs conditions for safe and legal abortion.

stem: At a PHC, a medical officer provides treatment and counseling to diabetic patients. Their body weights are recorded before the intervention and again 6 months later, using the health register. Which of the following statistical tests is most appropriate to analyze the change in weight in this group?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• A Paired t-test (also called dependent t-test) is used when:
1. The same group of subjects is measured before and after an intervention.
2. The variable of interest (here, body weight) is quantitative and normally distributed.
• It compares means of two related samples to see if the difference is statistically significant. (Note: Option D 'Dependent t-test' is a synonym, but 'Paired t-test' is the standard term favored in the provided key).

Other Options:
Option A: Chi-square test
Chi-square test is used for categorical data, not continuous variables like weight.
Option C: ANOVA test
ANOVA test is used when comparing means across more than 2 groups, not just two related measurements.
Option D: Dependent t-test
Dependent t-test is synonymous with Paired t-test, but the exact standard term requested is Paired t-test.

stem: In statistics, what do centiles and quartiles primarily represent?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Centiles (percentiles) divide data into 100 equal parts, showing relative standing. Quartiles divide ordered data into 4 parts (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4).
• They help determine the position of an individual value in the dataset.
• Useful in ranking, exam scores, growth charts, and income distribution studies.
• Unlike mean or variance, they indicate placement rather than overall summary.

Other Options:
Option A: Measures of central tendency
Measures of central tendency include mean, median, mode.
Option B: Measures of dispersion
Measures of dispersion describe the spread or variability (range, variance, standard deviation).
Option D: Measures of correlation
Measures of correlation assess relationships between variables.

stem: Which of the following is an incorrect match of Vector/Agent-Disease Caused?
subject: Microbiology, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Scrub typhus is caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi, not directly by the mite. The vector is the chigger (larval stage of mite), which transmits the infection.
• Here, 'Mite - Scrub typhus' is incorrectly matched if written as the causal agent rather than the vector in a mixed list of agents and vectors. (This is a classic MCQ trap mixing up agent vs. vector). Correct match should be: Agent - Orientia tsutsugamushi; Vector - Mite (chigger).

Other Options:
Option A: Louse - Epidemic typhus
Epidemic typhus is transmitted by the Human body louse.
Option B: Rickettsia - Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever
Rickettsia rickettsii is the agent for RMSF.
Option D: Poxvirus - Molluscum contagiosum
Molluscum contagiosum virus (a Poxvirus) is the agent causing the disease.

stem: In a village of 100 children, 10 children have a past history of measles (i.e., they are not at risk now), 20 new cases of measles were reported this year. What is the incidence of measles in this population for the year?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Incidence = (New cases / Population at risk) x 100.
• New cases = 20. Population at risk = Total population - immune individuals = 100 - 10 = 90.
• Incidence = (20 / 90) x 100 = 22.2%.

Other Options:
Option A: 20%
20% ignores the concept of 'population at risk' (dividing by 100 instead of 90).
Option C: 25%
25% overestimates the true incidence, reflecting a miscalculation of the at-risk population (e.g., dividing by 80).
Option D: 18%
18% underestimates the incidence and does not align with the epidemiological formula.

stem: Which of the following pairs is incorrectly matched with respect to basic statistical concepts?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Mean and median are measures of central tendency, not dispersion.
• Dispersion is described by range, variance, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation.
• Confusing central tendency with variability is a common exam trap. Both mean and median summarize the 'center' of data, not its spread. Hence, this pair is incorrectly matched.

Other Options:
Option A: Standard error - Variability
Standard error measures the variability of a sample statistic.
Option B: Correlation coefficient - Relationship
Correlation coefficient quantifies the strength and direction of the relationship between two variables.
Option D: Moments - Skewness
Moments describe the shape of a distribution (the third moment about mean gives skewness).

stem: Which of the following is not Grievous hurt as per 116 BNS?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
• Grievous hurt (Sec. 116 BNS, earlier Sec. 320 IPC) includes emasculation, permanent loss of sight/hearing, disfigurement of head/face, fracture/dislocation of bone or tooth, and pain preventing daily activities for 20 days or more.
• Severe pain for 5 days does not qualify, since the minimum required duration is 20 days.
• Therefore, this option is not grievous hurt.

Other Options:
Option A: Elbow dislocation
Elbow dislocation is a dislocation of a bone/joint, explicitly classified as grievous hurt.
Option C: Emasculation
Emasculation is defined as permanent loss of reproductive ability in a male and is grievous hurt.
Option D: Broken tooth
Broken tooth falls under fracture/dislocation of bone or tooth and is legally grievous hurt.

stem: Following is wrong statement?
options: Hyaluronidase is common content of snake venom
subject: FMT, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Elapidae (Cobra, Krait) produce neurotoxic venom, not myotoxic. Neurotoxins act at the neuromuscular junction, causing respiratory paralysis.
• Myotoxicity is seen more with sea snake (Hydrophidae) venoms.
• Hence, the statement that Elapidae bites are myotoxic is incorrect.

Other Options:
Option A: Vipers are hemotoxic
Viperidae (Russell's viper, Saw-scaled viper) venom is vasculotoxic/hemotoxic.
Option C: Cobra belong to Elapidae
Cobra and Krait are classic members of the Elapidae family.
Option D: Hyaluronidase is common content of snake venom
Hyaluronidase is known as a 'spreading factor' present in almost all poisonous snake venoms.

stem: A 40 year old comes with her daughter to hospital with recent history of rape offence. Mother is claiming her daughter to be less than 18 years but doctor does not agree. Which X ray used to prove age more than 18 years?
options: Appearance of Inner end clavicle, Knee joint & Wrist Joint
Fusion of Ischial tuberosity, lower end of Humerus
Medial end of Clavicle and Sternum
Medial end of Clavicle and Skull
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
• Estimating if a person is over 18 years involves examining specific ossification centers.
• The appearance of the inner (medial) end of the clavicle, along with fusion patterns around the knee joint and wrist, helps in establishing age around 18 years.
• The medial end of the clavicle is one of the last bones to ossify (appearing around 18-21 years and fusing later).

Other Options:
Option B: Fusion of Ischial tuberosity, lower end of Humerus
The lower end of the humerus fuses much earlier (around 14-16 years).
Option C: Medial end of Clavicle and Sternum
Complete fusion of the medial end of the clavicle and sternum occurs later (around 22-25 years).
Option D: Medial end of Clavicle and Skull
Skull sutures fuse much later in life and are not precise for determining an 18-year threshold.

stem: According to the amended MTP act, opinion of two doctors needed in following gestational age?
subject: FMT, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• As per the Medical Termination of Pregnancy (Amendment) Act, 2021, up to 20 weeks, the opinion of one registered medical practitioner is sufficient.
• For 21-24 weeks, termination requires the opinion of two registered medical practitioners.
• Beyond 24 weeks, termination is permissible only in specific cases (e.g., fetal anomalies) with the approval of a medical board.

Other Options:
Option A: 10 to 12 weeks
Only one doctor's opinion is required up to 20 weeks.
Option B: 12 to 20 weeks
Still within the single doctor opinion requirement.
Option D: 6 to 10 weeks
Only one doctor's opinion is sufficient.

stem:
Match the following:
Column AColumn B
A. Lead1. Deliriant
B. Croton2. Organic irritant
C. Opium3. Metallic Irritant
D. Cocaine4. CNS depressant
subject: FMT, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Lead: Metallic Irritant (A-3)
• Croton: Organic Irritant (B-2)
• Opium: CNS depressant (C-4)
• Cocaine: Deliriant / CNS stimulant-hallucinogen (D-1)
• This is the standard classification in toxicology.

Other Options:
Option B: A-1, B-2, C-3, D-4
• Mistakenly classifies Lead as Deliriant (wrong, it is a metallic irritant).
• Incorrectly places Opium as Metallic Irritant
• Mix-up between agents and categories.
Option C: A-2, B-1, C-3, D-4
• Puts Lead as Organic Irritant (wrong, lead is metallic).
• Assigns Croton as Deliriant, which is incorrect.
• Opium again wrongly shifted to metallic irritant.
Option D: A-2, B-4, C-3, D-1
• Lead as Organic Irritant (wrong).
• Croton as CNS depressant (wrong, it's an irritant oil).
• Opium as Metallic Irritant (wrong).
• Only Cocaine Deliriant is correct here.

stem: Factor worker in thermometer industry, came with pink lesions. Following is seen in this Poisoning?
subject: FMT, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Thermometer industry worker with pink lesions suggests chronic mercury exposure.
• Chronic mercury poisoning causes pink disease (acrodynia: pinkish rash on hands/feet).
• Neurological feature: Erethism mercurialis, also known as 'Mad hatter's syndrome'. Characterized by irritability, depression, shyness, tremors, and memory loss.
• Classically described in hat-making industry workers and thermometer factory workers.

Other Options:
Option B: Mee's line
Mee's lines are seen in Arsenic poisoning (transverse white lines on nails).
Option C: Basophilic stippling
Basophilic stippling is characteristic of Lead poisoning due to defective heme synthesis.
Option D: Neuropathy
Neuropathy is classic in Arsenic, Lead, and Hexacarbon poisoning; mercury mainly causes tremors and behavioral changes rather than pure neuropathy.

stem: Medical data security comes under which law?
subject: FMT, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• DPDP Act, 2023 (Digital Personal Data Protection Act, India) regulates the collection, storage, and processing of personal digital data, including medical data.
• It ensures privacy, confidentiality, and security of patient information.
• Establishes rules for data fiduciaries and processors in handling sensitive data.
• Replaces earlier fragmented provisions with a comprehensive Indian law.
• Very high-yield as this is a newly introduced legal update for medical professionals.

Other Options:
Option A: HIPAA
• Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (USA, 1996).
• Governs health data privacy in the US, not in India.
• Wrong jurisdiction for Indian exams.
Option C: PC-PNDT
• Pre-Conception and Pre-Natal Diagnostic Techniques Act, 1994 (India).
• Focuses on prevention of female feticide and misuse of diagnostic techniques.
• Not a data security law.
Option D: THOTA
• Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues Act (India).
• Regulates organ donation and transplantation, not data security.

stem: Mechanism of Cyanide and Carbon monoxide is inhibition of?
subject: Biochemistry, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Cyanide and Carbon monoxide inhibit Cytochrome C oxidase (Complex IV) of the electron transport chain.
• This blocks the final step of oxidative phosphorylation, preventing transfer of electrons to oxygen.
Leads to histotoxic hypoxia-tissues cannot utilize oxygen even when present.
Causes lactic acidosis due to anaerobic metabolism.
• Responsible for rapid fatality in cyanide and severe CO poisoning.

Other Options:
Option A: NADH dehydrogenase
• Part of Complex I in the electron transport chain.
Inhibited by barbiturates, rotenone.
• Not the site of cyanide or CO action.
Option B: Succinate dehydrogenase
• Part of Complex II in the electron transport chain.
Inhibited by malonate.
• Not involved in mechanism of cyanide/CO poisoning
Option C: Cytochrome C oxidoreductase
• Refers to Complex III (Coenzyme -Cytochrome C reductase).
Inhibited by antimycin A.
• Not the target site of cyanide or CO.

stem: Calculate the surface area of burn injuries in below case of accidental burn while cooking involving front of chest, Abdomen, both upper limbs, face and eye brows?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
Rule of Nines (adult): Anterior trunk = 18% each upper limb = 9% anterior head/face = 4.5%
Front of chest (anterior trunk) taken as 18% in rapid field estimation.
Both upper limbs = 9% + 9% = 18%.
Face (anterior head) 4.5%; eyebrows are included in the facial area.
Total 18% + 18% + 4.5% = 40.5% -> falls within 40-45%.

Other Options:
Option A: 27 to 30%
• Would underestimate by omitting part(s) of involved regions (e.g., counting one arm only).
Inconsistent with Rule of Nines for anterior trunk + both arms + face.
• Not supported by standard burn area calculations.
Option B: 33 to 37%
• Near-miss that typically results from taking chest as 9% (incorrect) instead of 18%.
Still undercounts the true TBSA when both arms and face are included.
• Common calculation trap in exams.
Option D: 50 to 54%
• Would require adding extra regions (e.g., abdomen/back/legs), which are not involved here.
Overestimation relative to the described areas.
• Not aligned with rapid adult estimation methods.

stem: Triad of punctate laceration, bruise, and abrasion seen in?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
The triad of punctate laceration, bruise, and abrasion is characteristic of firearm entry wounds.
It results from the combined effects of the bullet and the gases expelled from the muzzle.
The punctate laceration is caused by the projectile tearing the skin.
The bruise (contusion collar) is produced by the impact of the bullet against the skin.
The abrasion collar results from the rubbing of the bullet as it enters the body, showing the direction of fire.

Other Options:
Option A: Blast Injuries
• Caused by pressure waves, shrapnel, and burns.
• Do not produce the specific triad seen in firearm wounds.
• May show multiple abrasions and lacerations but not the classic combination.
Option B: Road traffic accident
• Produces abrasions, lacerations, and contusions but not the specific punctate laceration with abrasion collar pattern.
Injuries are typically linear or brush-type.
Option D: Sharp Cut Injuries
• Mostly only laceration wound persist, not related to this triad.

stem: Identify the dental Charting system?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
In firearm wounds (close/near range), a characteristic triad is observed:
1. Punctate lacerations (due to pellets or fragments).
2. Bruises (contusions) from impact of projectiles.
3. Abrasions around the entry site.
This triad is diagnostic of firearm entry wounds in forensic medicine.
• Helps in differentiating firearm wounds from other trauma like blunt force or blast.
• Highly exam-relevant and high-yield MCQ.

Other Options:
Option A: Blast injuries
• Typically cause polytrauma: burns, lacerations, ruptured tympanic membrane, lung/abdominal injuries.
• Not characterized by this specific triad.
Injury pattern is wider and more varied.
Option B: Road traffic accident
Common injuries include abrasions, contusions, lacerations, fractures.
• Patterns like grazed abrasions, patterned bruises, crush injuries are more typical.
• Does not produce the triad described.
Option D: Firearm Injuries (repeated)
• Duplicate option - likely a printing/typing error.
• Only one correct answer is firearm injuries, already captured in Option C.

stem: Delayed-onset postoperative endophthalmitis is classically associated with?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
Delayed-onset postoperative endophthalmitis is classically associated with low-virulence organisms, especially Propionibacterium acnes.
Presents weeks to months after cataract surgery.
Characterized by chronic low-grade inflammation, sometimes with a white plaque on the posterior capsule.
Requires intraocular antibiotics +/- capsular bag removal for complete eradication.
• Distinct from acute, fulminant endophthalmitis, which occurs within days of surgery.

Other Options:
Option A: Staphylococcus aureus
• More common in acute-onset postoperative endophthalmitis.
• Causes rapid, severe infection, not delayed onset.
• Presents with pain, hypopyon, loss of vision.
Option C: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
• Highly virulent organism, associated with acute, devastating endophthalmitis.
• Leads to rapid loss of vision, panophthalmitis if untreated.
• Not responsible for chronic/delayed cases.
Option D: Candida albicans
• Causes endogenous endophthalmitis, especially in immunocompromised patients.
• Associated with systemic fungal infections, not delayed postoperative cases.

stem: A 26-year-old woman with multiple sclerosis presents with diplopia on lateral gaze. On examination, the right eye fails to adduct when looking left, while the left eye abducts with nystagmus. Convergence is preserved. Which of the following is the most likely site of lesion?
subject: Neurology, Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) results from a lesion in the MLF, which links the contralateral abducens nucleus with the ipsilateral oculomotor nucleus.
Causes defective adduction of the ipsilateral eye during horizontal gaze.
The contralateral eye abducts with nystagmus.
• Convergence remains intact since it bypasses the MLF.
• Common causes: Multiple sclerosis (young), pontine infarct (elderly).

Other Options:
Option B: Lesion of Abducens Nucleus
Leads to lateral rectus palsy with inability to abduct.
Both adduction and contralateral abduction are unaffected.
• Not typical of INO.
Option C: Lesion of Oculomotor Nucleus
• Produces complete 3rd nerve palsy: ptosis, mydriasis, eye 'down & out.'
• Adduction defect would be accompanied by multiple other deficits, unlike isolated INO.
Option D: Lesion of Trochlear Nerve
• Causes vertical diplopia (difficulty looking down & in).
• Classically worsens when going down stairs.
Not consistent with INO features.

stem: A 10-year-old boy presents with sudden onset of painful, red eyes associated with conjunctival hemorrhages and watery discharge. Several other children in his school have developed similar symptoms in the past week. Which of the following is the most common causative organism of acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis?
subject: Ophthalmology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis (AHC) is an epidemic conjunctivitis caused by Enterovirus 70.
• Characterized by sudden onset, pain, foreign body sensation, and subconjunctival hemorrhages.
Outbreaks are common in tropical regions, crowded settings, and schools.
The disease is highly contagious but self-limiting.
• Important public health ophthalmic condition.

Other Options:
Option A: Adenovirus
• Causes epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC) and pharyngoconjunctival fever.
• Features: follicles, keratitis, preauricular lymphadenopathy.
• Not primarily hemorrhagic.
Option C: Coxsackievirus A24
• Can also cause acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis, but less commonly than Enterovirus 70.
• Typically linked to smaller localized outbreaks.
• Exam favorite distractor.
Option D: Herpes simplex virus
• Causes herpetic keratoconjunctivitis.
• Presents with dendritic ulcers, vesicles, corneal involvement.
Not epidemic hemorrhagic conjunctivitis.

stem: A 45-year-old woman with long-standing diabetes presents for routine eye check-up. On examination, her best corrected visual acuity is <6/60 in the better eye and <3/60 in the worse eye. According to the National Programme for Control of Blindness (NPCB, India), which of the following definitions correctly describes blindness?
options: Visual acuity <6/18 in better eye with best correction
Visual acuity <6/60 in better eye with best correction
Visual acuity <3/60 in better eye with best correction
Visual acuity <6/24 in worse eye with best correction
subject: PSM, Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• As per NPCB (India) definition:
Blindness = Visual acuity <3/60 in the better eye with best possible correction.
It may also include:
• Visual field <= 10° in better eye.
In this patient:
Better eye = <6/60
Worse eye = <3/60
Since blindness is defined based on the better eye, and the threshold is <3/60, option C is correct.
• NPCB definition differs from WHO definitions used for global comparisons.
• Therefore, the correct NPCB definition is <3/60 in the better eye.

Other Options:
Option A: Visual acuity <6/18 in better eye with best correction
• Defines visual impairment, not blindness.
• Incorrect.
Option B: Visual acuity <6/60 in better eye with best correction
• Defines low vision, not blindness under NPCB.
• Incorrect.
Option D: Visual acuity <6/24 in worse eye with best correction
• Blindness is determined by the better eye, not the worse eye.
• Incorrect.

stem: A 28-year-old man undergoes penetrating keratoplasty following a corneal scar due to healed keratitis. The surgeon discusses the medium used for donor corneal preservation and the preferred suture material for corneal transplantation. Which of the following is correct?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
MK medium (McCarey-Kaufman medium) is widely used for short-term preservation (up to 4 days) of corneal tissue.
Nylon (10-0 monofilament) is the suture of choice in corneal transplantation because it is inert, non-absorbable, and causes minimal tissue reaction.
Ensures optimal wound apposition and minimal astigmatism after keratoplasty.
• High-yield question for eye banking and keratoplasty topics.
• Also tested in context of community ophthalmology (NPCB corneal blindness).

Other Options:
Option A: Optisol medium - Silk suture
• Optisol GS is used for long-term preservation (up to 2 weeks).
Silk sutures are not used in corneal surgery due to risk of tissue reaction and infection.
Option C: Ringer lactate - Catgut suture
Ringer lactate is not a preservation medium for cornea.
• Catgut sutures are absorbable and cause significant tissue reaction, unsuitable for cornea.
Option D: Balanced salt solution - Prolene suture
Balanced salt solution is an irrigating solution in intraocular surgery, not for corneal storage.
• Prolene (polypropylene) sutures are sometimes used but Nylon is preferred for corneal grafting.

stem: A 35-year-old man sustains an orbital injury following a road traffic accident. On examination, he has loss of sensation over the upper eyelid, cornea, and conjunctiva. Which of the following nerves provides the sensory supply to the orbit?
subject: Anatomy, Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
The trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V) is the main sensory nerve of the orbit.
• Sensory branches include:
1. Ophthalmic division (V1); via nasociliary, frontal, and lacrimal nerves.
2. Maxillary division (V2); via infraorbital and zygomatic branches.
• Responsible for corneal sensation, eyelid sensation, conjunctiva, and orbital periosteum.
• Vital for corneal reflex (afferent pathway).
Damage causes loss of corneal reflex and anesthesia in orbital distribution.

Other Options:
Option A: Facial nerve
Supplies motor innervation to facial muscles (including orbicularis oculi for eyelid closure).
• Provides parasympathetic secretomotor fibers to lacrimal gland.
• Not the sensory nerve of orbit.
Option C: Oculomotor nerve
• Supplies motor innervation to most extraocular muscles.
• Carries parasympathetic fibers for pupillary constriction and accommodation.
• No sensory function in orbit.
Option D: Trochlear nerve
• Purely motor nerve, supplies superior oblique muscle.
No sensory component.
• Not involved in corneal or eyelid sensation.

stem: A 55-year-old diabetic patient presents with sudden decrease in central vision. The ophthalmologist suspects macular edema and wants to visualize the individual layers of the retina to confirm the diagnosis. Which of the following investigations is most appropriate for detailed evaluation of retinal layers?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
OCT provides high-resolution cross-sectional imaging of the retina.
It uses low-coherence interferometry with infrared light to map different retinal layers.
Gold standard for diagnosing macular edema, macular hole, epiretinal membrane, retinal nerve fiber layer thinning.
• Non-invasive and repeatable test for monitoring diabetic macular edema and glaucoma.
• Considered the most advanced retinal imaging tool in routine ophthalmology practice.

Other Options:
Option A: Fundus Fluorescein Angiography (FFA)
• Provides vascular imaging of the retina and choroid.
• Good for neovascularization and leakage, but cannot detail retinal layers.
Option C: B-scan Ultrasonography
• Useful for posterior segment imaging when media is opaque (e.g., vitreous hemorrhage, cataract).
• Gives only gross retinal/choroidal anatomy, not individual retinal layers.
Option D: Visual Evoked Potential (VEP)
• Records electrical response of the visual cortex to light stimulus.
• Used to test optic nerve pathway function, not retinal layers.

stem: A 50-year-old male came to the ENT OPD with intense right sided neck pain for 1 day. The pain was seen to increase on deglutition and had no relieving factors. He also gave a history associated with ear pain on the right side. On laryngoscopy a fish bone was found to be impacted in the right pyriform fossa. Irritation of which nerve will cause pain?
options: External branch of superior laryngeal nerve
Internal branch of superior laryngeal nerve
subject: ENT, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The pyriform fossa is supplied by the internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve (branch of vagus).
This nerve provides sensory innervation to the mucosa of the pyriform fossa, laryngeal inlet, and supraglottic region.
Irritation (like fish bone impaction) causes sharp throat pain with referred otalgia (via auricular branch of vagus).
• Explains why foreign bodies in pyriform fossa can mimic ear pain.
• Very important clinical scenario frequently tested in exams.

Other Options:
Option A: External branch of superior laryngeal nerve
• Supplies cricothyroid muscle (motor).
Responsible for tensing vocal cords, not sensory to pyriform fossa.
• Not involved in pain perception here.
Option C: Glossopharyngeal nerve
• Provides sensation to posterior 1/3 of tongue, tonsil, oropharynx.
• Impaction in tonsillar fossa would involve this nerve, not pyriform fossa.
Option D: Hypoglossal nerve
• Purely motor nerve to tongue muscles.
• No sensory role in pharynx or pyriform fossa.

stem: Which among the following is a false statement regarding juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Biopsy under local anesthesia is contraindicated in JNA due to high vascularity and risk of profuse hemorrhage.
• Diagnosis is made by clinical features + imaging (CT/MRI/angiography).
Treatment is surgical excision +/- preoperative embolization.
• Hence, biopsy is the false statement.

Other Options:
Option A: Epistaxis
Recurrent, profuse epistaxis is the most common presenting feature.
• Occurs due to fragile vascular tumor in nasopharynx.
• Correct feature.
Option B: Involves adolescents
• Classically affects adolescent males (10-20 years).
• Strong association with hormonal (androgen) influence.
Correct feature.
Option C: Unilateral nasal obstruction
• Along with epistaxis, progressive unilateral nasal obstruction is a hallmark symptom.
• Caused by mass occupying nasopharynx + nasal cavity.
• Correct feature.

stem: A 30 year old male patient complained of sudden rotatory sensation vertigo which he experienced suddenly after waking up from the side of bed. The vertigo lasted for 1-2 mins and was associated with an episode of vomiting. There was no complaints of hearing loss, tinnitus, aural fullness.
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
BPPV is characterized by:
1. Sudden, short-lasting vertigo precipitated by change in head position (e.g., getting out of bed).
2. Duration usually seconds to 1-2 minutes.
3. Associated with nausea/vomiting but no auditory symptoms (hearing loss, tinnitus, fullness absent).
4. Caused by otolith displacement (canalithiasis) in posterior semicircular canal.
5. Confirmed by Dix-Hallpike test and treated with Epley's maneuver.

Other Options:
Option B: Meniere's disease
• Features episodic vertigo, fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness.
• Each attack lasts 20 minutes to hours, not seconds to minutes.
• Not consistent with this case.
Option C: Vestibular neuritis
• Presents with sudden severe vertigo lasting days, often post-viral.
• No auditory symptoms, but vertigo is prolonged, unlike brief BPPV.
• Not positional.
Option D: Vestibular schwannoma
• Presents with progressive unilateral sensorineural hearing loss +/- tinnitus, imbalance.
• Vertigo is typically chronic and mild, not acute, positional, or short-lasting.
• MRI needed for diagnosis.

stem: Which among the following patients is most eligible for undergoing a cochlear implant as per the candidacy guidelines?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
• Cochlear implant is indicated in patients with severe to profound bilateral sensorineural hearing loss who derive little or no benefit from hearing aids.
• Most eligible: children with congenital (prelingual) sensorineural hearing loss, where early implantation allows speech and language development.
Postlingual profound deafness (e.g., after meningitis, trauma, ototoxicity) is also an indication, but congenital cases are the classical candidates.
• Requires intact auditory nerve and central auditory pathways.
• Early intervention gives best rehabilitation outcomes.

Other Options:
Option B: Malleus fixation
• Causes conductive hearing loss, best managed by surgery (ossiculoplasty/stapedectomy).
• Cochlear implant is not indicated.
Option C: Otosclerosis
• Causes stapedial fixation -> conductive hearing loss.
• Treated by stapedectomy with prosthesis.
• CI may be considered only in rare advanced cochlear otosclerosis with severe SNHL, not routine.
Option D: Ototoxicity
Can cause sensorineural hearing loss.
Patients may be considered for CI if bilateral profound loss occurs.
Still, congenital deafness remains the most classical and eligible indication.

stem: History of a child who presented with symptoms of recent infection of ear associated with lock jaw and spasms of limbs. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics, ENT, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Tetanus can occur when Clostridium tetani spores enter through an infected wound, including infected middle ear or chronic suppurative otitis media.
Classic features: trismus (lockjaw), generalized muscle spasms, opisthotonus, and risus sardonicus.
The association of ear infection + trismus + limb spasms points to otogenic tetanus.
It is rare but life-threatening, requiring antitoxin, antibiotics, and supportive care.
• Important to differentiate from intracranial complications of ear infection.

Other Options:
Option A: Bezold abscess
• A deep neck abscess arising from mastoiditis.
• Causes neck swelling, fever, torticollis, but not trismus and spasms.
Option B: Meningitis
• Complication of otitis media, presents with fever, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia.
• Does not cause lockjaw or limb spasms.
Option D: Otogenic brain abscess
• Presents with fever, headache, vomiting, seizures, focal neurological deficits.
• No trismus or generalized spasms.

stem: A patient was given vecuronium and has to be reversed due to a complication. Which of the following should be used?
subject: Pharmacology, Anesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• Vecuronium is a non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocker.
• Its action can be reversed by acetylcholinesterase inhibitors such as Neostigmine.
• Neostigmine increases acetylcholine concentration at the neuromuscular junction, which competitively antagonizes the blockade.
Given along with anticholinergic (Atropine or Glycopyrrolate) to prevent muscarinic side effects (bradycardia, salivation).
• This is the standard reversal method for non-depolarizing muscle relaxants.

Other Options:
Option A: Atropine
• An anticholinergic drug.
• Used with neostigmine to counter muscarinic side effects, but cannot reverse vecuronium alone.
Option B: Dantrolene
• Drug of choice for malignant hyperthermia.
• Acts by reducing calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum.
• Not a reversal agent for neuromuscular blockers.
Option D: Baclofen
GABA-B agonist, used in spasticity management.
• Not related to neuromuscular blockade reversal.

stem: RTA with GCS 8/15. pt has tachycardia SBP-90 mmHg, SpO2-85% with breath sounds absent on the right side on auscultation. Best management?
options: Secure airway, IV Fluids, Insert ICD
IV fluids, Blood transfusion and chest x ray
subject: Surgery, Anesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• The patient has low GCS (<= 8) -> airway protection required (intubation).
• Absent breath sounds + hypoxia + hypotension strongly suggests right-sided tension pneumothorax/hemothorax.
Immediate management:
1. Secure airway (intubation + oxygenation).
2. IV fluids for shock.
3. Intercostal chest drain (ICD) insertion for pneumothorax/hemothorax.
• Definitive imaging (CT head/abdomen) can be done only after stabilization.
This follows the ATLS protocol (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure).

Other Options:
Option B: CT head and CECT abdomen
• Wrong as first step -> patient is unstable.
• CT requires stability; otherwise patient may deteriorate on the table.
Option C: IV fluids, Blood transfusion and chest x ray
• X-ray is not appropriate before emergency ICD placement in suspected pneumothorax/hemothorax.
• Treatment must not be delayed for imaging.
Option D: CT head and referral
• Not suitable in an unstable trauma patient.
Stabilization must precede referral or imaging.

stem: Which one is correct regarding lumbar puncture?
options: Post-dural puncture headache is more commonly seen in females
Lumbar puncture is done at L1-L2 interspace in adults
Post-dural puncture headache is worse in lying down position
Spinal cord ends at S1 level in adults
subject: Medicine, Anesthesia
explanation:
Solution
• Post-dural puncture headache (PDPH) is more common in females, pregnant women, and young adults due to hormonal and dural factors.
PDPH is worse in sitting/standing position and relieved in lying down position.
Lumbar puncture in adults is typically performed at L3-L4 or L4-L5 interspace, below the termination of spinal cord (L1-L2 in adults).
• Spinal cord ends at L1 in adults and at L3 in children.

Other Options:
Option B: Lumbar puncture is done at L1-L2 interspace in adults
Wrong -> risk of spinal cord injury.
• Correct site: L3-L4 or L4-L5 in adults.
Option C: Post-dural puncture headache is worse in lying down position
• Wrong -> it is relieved in lying down and worsens in upright position.
Option D: Spinal cord ends at S1 level in adults
Wrong -> in adults spinal cord ends at L1, in children at L3.
S1 is far too low.

stem: A patient on mechanical ventilation for ARDS suddenly develops tachycardia, hypotension, desaturation, and absent breath sounds on one side. Chest is hyperresonant on percussion. Which of the following statements is correct regarding this condition (tension pneumothorax)?
options: Immediate chest X-ray must be done before intervention
Treatment is needle decompression followed by Intercostal chest drain insertion
PEEP must be increased to correct hypoxemia
This is a non-emergency condition and can be managed conservatively
subject: Medicine, Anesthesia, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
Tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening emergency, especially in ventilated patients.
Immediate management: needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line) -> followed by intercostal chest drain (ICD) insertion in 5th intercostal space, midaxillary line.
Chest X-ray is not required before emergency decompression -> waiting for imaging delays treatment.
• Increasing PEEP worsens pneumothorax and is contraindicated.
It is an emergency, not a condition for conservative management.

Other Options:
Option A: Immediate chest X-ray must be done before intervention
Wrong -> imaging is not needed before life-saving decompression.
• Diagnosis is clinical in unstable patients.
Option C: PEEP must be increased to correct hypoxemia
• Wrong -> PEEP worsens air trapping and pressure in pneumothorax.
• Contraindicated.
Option D: This is a non-emergency condition and can be managed conservatively
Wrong -> tension pneumothorax is a medical emergency.
• Delayed treatment can cause cardiac arrest.

stem: A 35-year-old male presents with a painless, progressive lesion over the neck. No systemic symptoms. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
subject: Dermatology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• The image and history are classical for Lupus vulgaris (cutaneous tuberculosis) -> a chronic, progressive plaque with scarring and atrophy.
• The most appropriate next investigation is skin biopsy, which shows tuberculoid granulomas with caseating necrosis and Langhans giant cells.
• Biopsy confirms diagnosis of cutaneous TB.
• Other supportive investigations: Mantoux test, chest X-ray, mycobacterial culture, PCR for MTB.
Important for definitive diagnosis and guiding treatment.

Other Options:
Option A: KOH mount
Used to detect fungal hyphae in dermatophytosis, candidiasis.
Not useful in suspected cutaneous TB.
Option C: HIV ELISA
• Useful for immunosuppression screening, but not diagnostic of the skin lesion.
• May be done later if disseminated TB or atypical features are present.
Option D: Gram stain
• Detects bacterial infections like staphylococcal/streptococcal pyoderma.
• Not helpful for mycobacterial infections.

stem: A young man presents with painful grouped vesicles on the penis. Histopath shows multinucleated giant cells. What is the drug of choice?
subject: Pharmacology, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• The description is classical for Genital Herpes (Herpes simplex virus infection).
• Features: painful grouped vesicles -> shallow ulcers -> healing with crusting.
Tzanck smear shows multinucleated giant cells.
• Acyclovir is the drug of choice; alternatives include Valacyclovir, Famciclovir.
• Early initiation reduces duration, severity, recurrence.
• Important STD-related dermatology MCQ.

Other Options:
Option B: Valacyclovir
• Also effective against HSV, but Acyclovir remains the first-line drug of choice in most guidelines (especially exam perspective).
Option C: Azithromycin
• Used in bacterial STDs like chlamydia, chancroid.
• No role in viral infections like HSV.
Option D: Penicillin
• Drug of choice for syphilis (Treponema pallidum).
• Not useful in herpes.

stem: A patient diagnosed with gonorrhea shows resistance to penicillin. What is the drug of choice?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Neisseria gonorrhoeae has developed widespread resistance to penicillin, tetracyclines, and fluoroquinolones.
• The CDC and WHO recommend ceftriaxone (250-500 mg IM single dose) as the drug of choice for uncomplicated gonorrhea.
Sometimes given with azithromycin or doxycycline to cover for possible chlamydia co-infection.
Safe, effective, and widely used in both genital and extragenital gonorrhea.

Other Options:
Option B: Ampicillin
• Similar mechanism to penicillin, and gonococcus is resistant.
• Not recommended.
Option C: Meropenem
• A broad-spectrum carbapenem, not recommended for routine gonorrhea treatment.
• Reserved for multidrug-resistant infections but not standard of care.
Option D: Vancomycin
• Used for Gram-positive organisms (e.g., MRSA, enterococci).
Ineffective against Gram-negative Neisseria.

stem: Which of the following are used for treatment of scabies in infants?
subject: Pharmacology, Pediatrics, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
Permethrin 5% cream is the first-line treatment for scabies in infants (>= 2 months old).
Safe, effective, and minimally absorbed through skin.
Applied overnight to the entire body (including scalp and face in infants).
Considered the drug of choice for all age groups including infants, children, and adults.
• Reapplication after 1 week is often recommended.

Other Options:
Option B: Benzyl benzoate 25%
• Effective, but irritant to skin and not preferred in infants and children.
• May cause severe dermatitis.
Option C: Crotamiton 10%
• Less effective compared to permethrin.
• Sometimes used, but not recommended as first-line, especially in infants.
Option D: Oral Ivermectin
• Effective systemic drug, but contraindicated in children <15 kg and infants due to neurotoxicity risk.
• Used in severe, crusted, or refractory scabies in older children/adults.

stem: A patient had a prior genital ulcer that healed on its own. Now presents with pigmented rash on palms and soles (image given). Most likely diagnosis?
subject: Dermatology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Classical presentation of secondary syphilis:
1. Follows a painless chancre (primary lesion) that heals spontaneously.
2. Symmetrical, non-itchy maculopapular rash on palms and soles.
3. May be accompanied by generalized lymphadenopathy, mucous patches, condyloma lata.
4. Rash heals with post-inflammatory pigmentation.
Highly infectious stage of syphilis.
Diagnosis confirmed by serology (VDRL, RPR, TPHA, FTA-ABS).

Other Options:
Option A: Fixed drug eruption
Produces round/oval erythematous patches after drug intake.
Lesions recur at the same site on re-exposure.
Not generalized, not involving palms and soles in this manner.
Option B: Tinea corporis
Presents with annular, scaly plaques with central clearing.
Localized fungal infection, not widespread pigmented rash.
Option D: Rocky Mountain spotted fever
Tick-borne disease (Rickettsia rickettsii).
Rash involves palms and soles, but patient history of genital ulcer is not consistent.
Systemic illness with fever, headache, myalgia is typical.

stem: A young patient presents with an asymptomatic, brown to black macule on the palm (image given). The lesion is suspected to be Tinea nigra. What is the investigation of choice?
subject: Dermatology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Tinea nigra is a superficial fungal infection caused by Hortaea werneckii (formerly Exophiala werneckii).
KOH mount of skin scrapings reveals dematiaceous (pigmented), septate hyphae.
Simple, rapid, and diagnostic -> hence the investigation of choice.
Culture can confirm but is not needed for routine diagnosis.
Wood's lamp is not useful as Tinea nigra does not fluoresce.

Other Options:
Option A: Wood's lamp examination
• Useful in some fungal infections (Microsporum, Malassezia).
Tinea nigra does not fluoresce, so not diagnostic.
Option C: Skin biopsy
Shows fungal hyphae in stratum corneum but is not first-line.
Reserved for doubtful cases.
Option D: Fungal culture
Can identify Hortaea werneckii on Sabouraud's dextrose agar, but takes weeks.
Used for confirmation, not primary diagnosis.

stem:
"Lord Hanuman was celibate. I am celibate. Hence, I am Hanuman"
This reasoning is an example of which thought disorder?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Autistic thinking refers to prelogical, subjective reasoning that does not follow normal logic.
• Patient draws conclusions based on personal beliefs, magical thinking, or illogical associations (e.g., identifying himself with Hanuman just because both are celibate).
• Commonly seen in schizophrenia and psychotic disorders.
Represents idiosyncratic, fantasy-based logic disconnected from reality.
Important distinguishing feature in psychotic thought disorders.

Other Options:
Option A: Neologism
Refers to newly coined words or phrases by the patient with idiosyncratic meaning.
Example: "I am suffering from flamitation."
Not applicable here.
Option B: Verbigeration
• Refers to meaningless repetition of words/phrases (speech stereotypy).
• Example: repeating "come, come, come" without context.
Not seen in this case.
Option D: Loss of association
• Refers to loosening of association of ideas, leading to disorganized or incoherent speech.
• Example: "The sun is bright. Bright light makes me happy. Happy birthday to you."
• Not demonstrated in the given reasoning.

stem: Which of the following new psychoactive substances has been introduced for the treatment of depressive disorder?
subject: Pharmacology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
Ketamine, an NMDA receptor antagonist, has shown rapid antidepressant action, especially in treatment-resistant depression.
• Acts by increasing glutamate transmission, unlike conventional SSRIs/SNRIs which take weeks.
• Esketamine (intranasal form) has received regulatory approval for resistant depression.
• Major update in biological psychiatry & psychopharmacology.
Important recent exam update tested in NEET PG & INI CET.

Other Options:
Option B: Cannabinoids
Investigated for chronic pain and anxiety, not approved for depression.
Option C: Haloperidol
A typical antipsychotic, used in schizophrenia and mania.
• No role in depression.
Option D: LSD
• A hallucinogen, studied in psychedelic therapy, but not approved for depression.

stem: Which of the following is not a part of the ICD 11 diagnosis for Major depressive disorder?
options: Loss of interest in pleasurable things
Low mood for most part of the day
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• According to ICD-11, the three core symptoms for major depressive disorder are:
1. Depressed mood most of the day, nearly every day.
2. Loss of interest/pleasure in activities (anhedonia).
3. Decreased energy or increased fatigability.
Additional symptoms may include disturbed sleep, decreased appetite, poor concentration, feelings of worthlessness/guilt, suicidal thoughts, etc.
Low self-esteem/confidence is emphasized in ICD-10, but not listed as a core ICD-11 criterion.
• Thus, the correct answer is not part of ICD-11.

Other Options:
Option A: Low energy
• Present in ICD-11 as one of the three core symptoms.
Option B: Loss of interest in pleasurable things
Also a core ICD-11 symptom (anhedonia).
Option C: Low mood for most part of the day
• The primary symptom required for diagnosis in ICD-11.

stem: Which of the following is used as first line agent for treatment of Acute mania?
subject: Pharmacology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
Acute mania requires rapid control of excitement, aggression, and psychotic symptoms.
• Antipsychotics (Haloperidol, Olanzapine, Risperidone) are considered first-line agents for acute control.
• Lithium and Valproate are excellent mood stabilizers but take several days to weeks to act, so they are not used for immediate control.
• Amitriptyline (a TCA antidepressant) can worsen mania, hence contraindicated.
Therefore, Haloperidol is the preferred choice in acute settings.

Other Options:
Option B: Lithium
Gold standard for maintenance treatment of bipolar disorder.
Too slow in onset for acute mania control.
Option C: Valproate
• Effective as a mood stabilizer and used in acute mania, but acts slower than antipsychotics.
Often combined with antipsychotics.
Option D: Amitriptyline
• Antidepressant (TCA), not used in mania.
• May trigger or worsen manic episodes.

stem: 40 year old woman with bipolar disorder planning a pregnancy. Which one will you avoid to avoid NTD?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
Valproate is strongly associated with teratogenicity, especially neural tube defects (spina bifida, meningomyelocele).
It is contraindicated in pregnancy, particularly during the first trimester.
Other risks: craniofacial defects, cardiac malformations, cognitive impairment in child.
In bipolar disorder, safer alternatives in pregnancy include Lamotrigine (preferred) or antipsychotics.
• Thus, Valproate must be avoided in women planning pregnancy.

Other Options:
Option B: Oxcarbamazepine
• Structurally related to carbamazepine, some teratogenic risk but lower than valproate.
• Not first choice, but less harmful.
Option C: Lamotrigine
• Considered the safest mood stabilizer in pregnancy.
• Preferred alternative to valproate/lithium.
Option D: Levetiracetam
• Antiepileptic with relatively low teratogenic risk.
Sometimes used in women of reproductive age.

stem: All are correct except?
options: Buprenorphine is used for opioid withdrawal management
Flumazenil is used for LONG TERM management for ADS
Cannabis withdrawal has limited withdrawal symptoms
subject: Psychiatry, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Flumazenil is a benzodiazepine receptor antagonist, used for acute benzodiazepine overdose, not for long-term management of alcohol dependence.
• Long-term management of ADS is done with naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram.
Hence, this statement is incorrect.

Other Options:
Option A: Opioids withdrawal is rarely fatal
• Correct -> though withdrawal causes severe distress (pain, diarrhea, vomiting, lacrimation, rhinorrhea), it is not life-threatening, unlike alcohol/benzo withdrawal.
Option B: Buprenorphine is used for opioid withdrawal management
• Correct - partial opioid agonist, used for detoxification and maintenance in opioid dependence.
Option D: Cannabis withdrawal has limited withdrawal symptoms
Correct -> symptoms are mild, including irritability, insomnia, anorexia, anxiety.
• Not life-threatening.

stem: Image of an MRI brain shows the "Eye of the Tiger" sign. This finding is most commonly seen in:
subject: Neurology, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
"Eye of the Tiger" sign is a characteristic MRI finding on T2-weighted imaging.
It shows a central hyperintensity with surrounding hypointensity in the globus pallidus.
Seen in Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN), part of the Neurodegeneration with Brain Iron Accumulation (NBIA) group.
Caused by mutations in the PANK2 gene.
• Associated with progressive dystonia, parkinsonism, and pigmentary retinopathy.

Other Options:
Option B: Wilson's disease
MRI shows "Face of the Giant Panda sign" in the midbrain, not "Eye of the Tiger."
Option C: Huntington's chorea
MRI shows caudate nucleus atrophy with enlarged frontal horns of lateral ventricles.
Option D: Krabbe's disease
MRI shows diffuse white matter changes (leukodystrophy), not basal ganglia findings.

stem: The following serial radiographic images (shown) demonstrate the evaluation of anorectal function during defecation. The modality used here is:
subject: Radiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Defecography (also called evacuation proctography) is a dynamic fluoroscopic X-ray study performed after instilling contrast paste (barium) into the rectum.
Used to evaluate anorectal angle, rectocele, intussusception, rectal prolapse, and pelvic floor dysfunction during straining/defecation.
The images shown are X-ray fluoroscopic sequences during defecation.
MR defecography is an alternative (non-radiation, multiplanar imaging), but the given images are classical X-ray defecogram.

Other Options:
Option A: MR defecography
Uses MRI instead of X-ray.
Provides better soft tissue detail but not what is shown here.
Option C: Barium swallow
Evaluates esophagus and upper GI tract, not anorectal region.
Option D: CT defecogram
Uses CT with contrast, less commonly used due to high radiation.
Not the modality in the given image.

stem: A skull X-ray image shows an osteolytic lesion consistent with skull metastasis. Which of the following thyroid malignancies is most commonly associated with skull metastasis?
subject: Surgery, Radiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Follicular carcinoma thyroid commonly spreads via the hematogenous route, unlike papillary carcinoma (which spreads lymphatically).
The most frequent sites of blood-borne metastases: bones (especially skull and vertebrae), lungs, and liver.
Hence, skull metastasis is a classical radiological feature of follicular carcinoma.
Appears as expansile lytic lesions on skull X-ray/CT.

Other Options:
Option B: Papillary carcinoma thyroid
• Most common thyroid cancer overall.
Spreads by lymphatics -> cervical lymph node metastases, not bone.
Option C: Thyroid lymphoma
• Rare, associated with Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
• Presents with rapid neck swelling, not bone metastasis.
Option D: Hurthle cell carcinoma
• Variant of follicular neoplasm.
• Metastasis can occur but less commonly to skull compared to follicular carcinoma.

stem: Which of the following imaging modalities is contraindicated in pregnancy?
subject: Radiology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• CT (Computed Tomography) uses ionizing radiation, which poses risks of teratogenicity, growth restriction, and childhood malignancies when used during pregnancy.
Hence, contraindicated unless absolutely essential (e.g., trauma, pulmonary embolism where benefits outweigh risks).
USG and Doppler are completely safe -> no ionizing radiation.
MRI is considered safe (no radiation), though first trimester use is limited and gadolinium contrast is avoided.

Other Options:
Option A: USG
Safe, first-line modality in pregnancy.
• No radiation exposure.
Option B: MRI
Safe in pregnancy, especially second and third trimester.
• Gadolinium contrast is avoided.
Option D: Doppler
• Safe and commonly used in obstetric evaluation (umbilical artery, MCA, uterine artery flow).

stem: A 55-year-old woman with low back pain undergoes a bone scan. The image shows the classical Honda sign (H-sign) in the sacrum (marked by arrows). What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Radiology, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
The Honda sign (H-sign) on bone scan is pathognomonic for sacral insufficiency fractures.
Appears as bilateral vertical uptake in sacral ala + horizontal uptake in the sacral body -> resembling an H/Honda logo.
Typically seen in elderly, postmenopausal osteoporotic women.
Bone scan (Tc-99m MDP) is highly sensitive for detecting this fracture.
MRI can further confirm the diagnosis.

Other Options:
Option B: Multiple myeloma
Causes lytic bone lesions.
May not always show increased uptake on bone scan -> bone scan often underestimates myeloma lesions.
Option C: Transient bone marrow edema
Seen on MRI, not bone scan.
Self-limiting condition.
Option D: Acute osteomyelitis
Shows focal increased uptake, but not Honda sign.

stem: A male patient presents with renal colic. Which of the following is the investigation of choice for detecting a ureteric stone?
subject: Surgery, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
Non-contrast CT KUB (NCCT KUB) is the gold standard investigation for suspected ureteric stones.
It has highest sensitivity and specificity (>95%) for detecting all types of calculi (including radiolucent uric acid and cystine stones).
Provides information about size, site, number, density of stones, and also identifies alternative diagnoses.
Quick, widely available, and non-invasive.

Other Options:
Option B: USG KUB
• First-line in pregnant women and children (no radiation).
Less sensitive for small ureteric stones.
Option C: X-ray KUB
• Detects only radio-opaque stones (calcium oxalate, phosphate).
• Misses radiolucent stones (uric acid, xanthine).
Option D: Ureteroscopy
• A therapeutic procedure, not a primary diagnostic tool.
• Used after diagnosis for stone removal.

stem: A child presents with a history of recurrent seizures without fever. MRI brain shows a single ring-enhancing lesion in the right temporal lobe (Image given). What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics, Neurology, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
In children from endemic areas (India, Latin America, Southeast Asia), the most common cause of solitary ring-enhancing lesion with seizures is neurocysticercosis (NCC).
Typically presents with seizures in a child/young adult, afebrile, with a small (<20 mm), well-defined, ring-enhancing lesion.
The scolex may be visible inside the lesion (pathognomonic).
MRI is more sensitive than CT for identifying scolex and cyst stage.
Brain abscess and tuberculoma are differentials but usually have clinical/systemic features.

Other Options:
Option B: Brain abscess
• Usually presents with fever, raised ICP, leukocytosis.
• Ring lesion is typically thicker and more irregular.
Option C: Tuberculoma
Common in endemic regions but often larger, irregular, multiple lesions.
May show T2 hypointensity due to caseating granulomas.
More common in adolescents and adults.
Option D: Metastasis
Solitary metastasis in children is rare.
More often multiple lesions with known primary.

stem: A 26-year-old male presents with 8 months history of bloody diarrhea. CT abdomen shows a rigid colon with loss of haustrae and pancolitis (image given). What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease with:
1. Continuous involvement, starting from the rectum and extending proximally (pancolitis in this case).
2. Loss of haustral pattern, producing the "lead-pipe colon" appearance.
3. Symptoms: bloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, urgency.
CT shows rigid colon, wall thickening, and loss of haustrae.
• Classic presentation of chronic UC with pancolitis.

Other Options:
Option A: Pseudomembranous colitis
• Caused by Clostridium difficile infection.
Imaging: accordion sign on CT due to mucosal edema, not rigid colon.
Option C: Crohn's disease
Characterized by skip lesions, cobblestoning, fistulas, strictures.
Does not usually cause continuous pancolitis with lead-pipe colon.
Option D: TB colitis
Involves ileocecal region predominantly.
Shows pulled-up contracted cecum and strictures.
Not diffuse pancolitis.

stem: Which type of pulse represents Frank-Starling law?
subject: Medicine, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Pulsus alternans is characterized by alternating strong and weak pulse beats, despite a regular rhythm.
• Represents the Frank-Starling mechanism: weak contraction leads to increased end-diastolic volume, which enhances the next beat.
Seen in severe left ventricular dysfunction.
Best detected by sphygmomanometer (Korotkoff sounds alternating in intensity) or arterial tracing.
• Classical association: advanced LV failure, dilated cardiomyopathy, severe hypertension.

Other Options:
Option A: Pulsus paradoxus
• Exaggerated fall (>10 mmHg) in systolic BP during inspiration.
• Seen in cardiac tamponade, severe asthma, constrictive pericarditis.
• Not related to Frank-Starling mechanism.
Option C: Pulsus parvus
Small, weak pulse seen in low cardiac output states like aortic stenosis, shock.
Option D: Pulsus et tardus
Slow-rising, delayed carotid pulse typical of severe aortic stenosis.

stem: What is the first/earliest symptom of Multiple Sclerosis (MS)?
subject: Neurology
explanation:
Solution
Sensory symptoms (paresthesias, numbness, tingling, "pins-and-needles") are the earliest and most common presentation of MS.
Occur due to demyelination of sensory pathways in CNS.
Symptoms often include loss of vibration sense, impaired position sense, and paresthesia in limbs.
• They may be transient, triggered by fever/stress (Uhthoff's phenomenon).
• These precede motor signs (ataxia, weakness) and cranial nerve involvement.

Other Options:
Option B: Vertigo
• May occur due to brainstem involvement, but not the earliest symptom.
Option C: Ataxia
Seen later due to cerebellar involvement, often a progressive finding.
Option D: Facial palsy
• Occurs if cranial nerves/brainstem lesions are affected; not usually the first presentation.

stem: An elderly patient presents with sudden onset headache, vomiting, and neck stiffness. On examination, Brudzinski's sign is positive. Imaging shows hydrocephalus but no hemorrhage. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Neurology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
