• Acute bacterial meningitis presents with sudden severe headache, fever, vomiting, photophobia, and meningeal signs (neck stiffness, Kernig's, Brudzinski's sign).
Imaging may show communicating hydrocephalus due to pus/inflammatory exudates blocking arachnoid villi. Importantly, no hemorrhage is seen on imaging.
• A medical emergency; requires prompt antibiotics + steroids.
• CSF analysis would confirm high protein, low glucose, neutrophilic pleocytosis.

Other Options:
Option A: Normal pressure hydrocephalus
• Presents with triad of gait disturbance, dementia, and urinary incontinence.
• No acute meningeal signs or headache.
Option B: Viral encephalitis
• Causes fever, altered mental status, seizures.
• Imaging shows parenchymal involvement (esp. temporal lobe in HSV), not isolated hydrocephalus.
Option D: Subarachnoid hemorrhage
Also presents with sudden severe headache (thunderclap) and meningismus.
• However, imaging would show blood in subarachnoid space, which is absent here.

stem: The murmur of Mitral Stenosis (MS) corresponds to which part of the ECG cycle?
subject: Medicine, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
The mid-diastolic rumbling murmur of mitral stenosis is best heard at the apex (left lateral position, bell of stethoscope).
It becomes louder (presystolic accentuation) just after the P-wave, when atrial contraction increases blood flow across the stenotic mitral valve.
Once atrial fibrillation develops, presystolic accentuation disappears (as atrial contraction is lost).
• Thus, the murmur of MS corresponds most closely to the P-wave on ECG.

Other Options:
Option B: QRS complex
• Represents ventricular depolarization.
• No direct correlation with MS murmur accentuation.
Option C: ST segment
• Represents ventricular systole/plateau phase.
• Not related to MS murmur.
Option D: T-wave
• Represents ventricular repolarization.
• No correlation with MS murmur changes.

stem: A patient was noted to have BP readings of 148/90 mmHg and 154/96 mmHg on two different occasions. As a medical officer, what is the next best step?
options: Start amlodipine for a month and follow up
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• BP values >= 140/90 mmHg on at least two occasions confirm Stage 1 Hypertension as per JNC-8/Indian guidelines.
The patient is asymptomatic and stable, so oral antihypertensives should be initiated.
Amlodipine (a calcium channel blocker) is a safe and effective first-line agent.
• Follow-up is required to assess response, compliance, and side effects.
• No indication for IV drugs or urgent referral here.

Other Options:
Option A: Observe
• Not correct, since persistent hypertension has been documented.
• Lifestyle advice alone is insufficient at this BP level.
Option B: Admit and give IV antihypertensive
• Reserved for hypertensive emergency (>180/120 with end-organ damage). This patient is stable with mild hypertension.
Option D: Refer to higher center
• Not required, as this can be managed at primary level with oral medication.

stem: Erythropoietin is not/least useful in the treatment of which of the following conditions?
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Nutritional anemia (Iron, B12, Folate deficiency) does not improve with erythropoietin, because substrates for RBC production are lacking.
The treatment requires replacement of the deficient nutrient (iron, folic acid, B12).
Giving EPO without correcting deficiency is ineffective.
• EPO is most effective when bone marrow is functional and adequate nutrition is present.
• Clinical use: Anemia of CKD, anemia due to chemotherapy, anemia in HIV patients on zidovudine.

Other Options:
Option B: Aplastic anemia
• Response is limited, but some effect may be seen if residual stem cells remain.
• Supportive therapy + bone marrow transplant are mainstays.
Option C: Anemia in myelofibrosis
• Marrow is fibrosed, so response to EPO is poor.
Supportive care, transfusion, and newer drugs (ruxolitinib) preferred.
Option D: Anemia of CKD
• Best indication for EPO therapy.
• Kidneys fail to produce endogenous EPO, so recombinant therapy is highly effective.

stem: Which of the following is the most common symptom of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)?
subject: Medicine, Rheumatology
explanation:
Solution
Arthralgia and myalgia are the most common presenting symptoms of SLE, seen in up to 80-90% of patients.
• Usually non-deforming, symmetric arthritis involving small joints.
These musculoskeletal symptoms often precede other systemic manifestations like nephritis or CNS involvement.
• They tend to persist and become more prominent as disease progresses.
• Important for early clinical suspicion when evaluating young females with polyarthralgia.

Other Options:
Option A: Malar rash
• Classic "butterfly rash" but seen in <50% at initial presentation.
• Not the most common feature.
Option C: Photosensitivity
Common feature but usually appears later in disease course.
• Often associated with rash and flare-ups.
Option D: Oral ulcers
• Painless ulcers, but not as frequent as arthralgia.
• Part of diagnostic ACR/EULAR criteria.

stem: Which of the following acid-base disorder is likely to be encountered in individuals ascending to high altitude?
subject: Physiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• At high altitude, decreased barometric pressure -> decreased pO2.
• Hypoxia stimulates peripheral chemoreceptors -> hyperventilation.
Hyperventilation leads to excessive CO2 washout -> low pCO2.
This produces a primary respiratory alkalosis.
With time, renal compensation occurs by excreting bicarbonate, helping restore pH.

Other Options:
Option A: Respiratory acidosis
• Occurs in hypoventilation (COPD, respiratory depression), not hyperventilation.
Option C: Metabolic acidosis
Seen in diarrhea, renal failure, lactic acidosis, not in altitude exposure.
Option D: Metabolic alkalosis
Seen in vomiting, diuretic use, mineralocorticoid excess.
• Not associated with hypoxia.

stem: Which of the following is NOT a feature of Heart Failure?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
In heart failure, raised JVP is typically pulsatile, reflecting elevated right atrial pressure transmitted via jugular veins.
• A non-pulsatile JVP suggests SVC obstruction, not heart failure. Therefore, this is not a feature of HF.

Other Options:
Option A: Orthopnea
• Dyspnea on lying flat due to pulmonary venous congestion.
• Classic feature of left heart failure.
Option B: PND (Paroxysmal Nocturnal Dyspnea)
• Sudden nocturnal breathlessness relieved by sitting up.
Seen in left ventricular failure.
Option C: Right hypochondriac pain
• Due to congestive hepatomegaly from right-sided HF.
• May be associated with tender enlarged liver.

stem: Which of the following is not a cause of recurrent renal stones?
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Citrate is a natural inhibitor of stone formation, as it binds calcium and prevents precipitation of calcium oxalate or calcium phosphate crystals.
Therefore, hypercitraturia does not cause stones.
Instead, hypocitraturia is a well-known risk factor for recurrent stone disease.
Common metabolic abnormalities in recurrent renal stones: hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria, hyperuricosuria, hypocitraturia.

Other Options:
Option A: Hyperoxaluria
Increases urinary oxalate, leading to calcium oxalate stones.
• Seen in dietary excess, intestinal malabsorption, primary hyperoxaluria.
Option B: Hypercalciuria
• Most common metabolic cause of recurrent renal stones.
• Can be due to absorptive, resorptive (hyperparathyroidism), or renal leak types.
Option D: Hyperuricosuria
Leads to formation of uric acid stones or acts as a nidus for calcium oxalate precipitation.
Seen in gout, high purine diet, myeloproliferative disorders.

stem: A child presented with cleft lip and planned for repair, which muscle will be repaired first during surgery?
subject: Surgery, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The orbicularis oris muscle is the primary muscle involved in cleft lip.
In cleft lip, fibers of orbicularis oris fail to unite across the midline.
• During surgical repair (e.g., Millard's rotation-advancement technique), re-approximation of orbicularis oris is the first and most critical step.
• Proper alignment restores oral sphincter function, aesthetics, and lip competence.
• Other muscles (like zygomaticus, levator alaeque nasi) are not primarily repaired.

Other Options:
Option B: Orbicularis oculi
• Muscle around the eye, unrelated to cleft lip repair.
Option C: Zygomaticus major
• Muscle of facial expression involved in smiling, not corrected in cleft lip surgery.
Option D: Levator alaeque nasi
• Elevates nasal ala, minor role in nasal deformity correction, but not the primary step in cleft lip repair.

stem: A 65-year-old patient is on mechanical ventilation for ARDS. Suddenly, the patient develops hypotension, tachycardia, absent breath sounds on the left side, and tracheal deviation to the right side. What is the likely cause?
options: Barotaruma due to high airway pressure
subject: Anesthesia, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
In a mechanically ventilated patient, sudden onset of hemodynamic collapse + unilateral absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation strongly suggests tension pneumothorax.
Barotrauma from high airway pressures can rupture alveoli -> air escapes into pleural space -> tension pneumothorax.
Clinical features: hypotension, tachycardia, unilateral absent breath sounds, tracheal shift, hyperresonant chest.
• Emergency management: Immediate needle decompression followed by Intercostal chest drain (ICD).

Other Options:
Option A: High tidal volume
Can contribute to ventilator-induced lung injury, but alone does not cause sudden tension pneumothorax.
Option C: ET tube malposition
If inserted into right main bronchus -> absent left breath sounds, but no tracheal deviation or shock.
Option D: Oxygen toxicity
• Occurs after prolonged high FiO2 exposure -> leads to ARDS-like picture, not acute tension pneumothorax.

stem: Which of these is the most life-threatening injury that can be identified by assessing the breathing component of the patient?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
In the primary survey of trauma (ABCDE approach), the B = Breathing step includes assessment for immediately life-threatening thoracic injuries.
Tension pneumothorax is the most rapidly fatal if unrecognized, as it causes: 1. Severe hypoxia from lung collapse 2. Reduced venous return due to mediastinal shift -> hypotension, shock 3. Absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, hyperresonant chest. It is a clinical diagnosis; management must be immediate needle decompression + chest tube.

Other Options:
Option A: Cervical spine injury
Life-threatening due to airway compromise but assessed under Airway (A), not Breathing.
Option C: Blunt cardiac injury
Serious, but detected under Circulation (C) assessment.
Option D: Laryngotracheal injury
• A critical airway problem, hence considered under Airway (A) evaluation.

stem: During laparoscopic cholecystectomy heaptocystic triangle is identified, which among the following is not the boundary of hepatocystic triangle?
subject: Surgery, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The hepatocystic triangle (also called Calot's triangle) is a key anatomical landmark during cholecystectomy.
Its boundaries are: 1. Medially: Common hepatic duct 2. Laterally: Cystic duct 3. Superiorly: Inferior surface of the liver.
• The cystic artery lies within the triangle, but it is not a boundary.
• Accurate identification of this triangle prevents bile duct injury during surgery.

Other Options:
Option A: Inferior edge of the liver
• Forms the superior boundary of Calot's (hepatocystic) triangle.
• Important reference landmark in laparoscopy.
Option B: Cystic duct
• Forms the lateral boundary of the triangle.
Joins the common hepatic duct to form the common bile duct.
Option D: Common hepatic duct
Forms the medial boundary of the triangle. Lies anterior to the portal vein.

stem: Identify the investigation performed in the image:
subject: Radiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
The image shows contrast-filled rectum with dynamic X-ray taken during defecation.
This is the defecography (evacuation proctography) test. It is performed using fluoroscopy with contrast enema to evaluate: 1. Rectocele 2. Intussusception 3. Rectal prolapse 4. Anismus (dyssynergic defecation). Helps in diagnosing pelvic floor disorders not detected on routine proctoscopy or colonoscopy.

Other Options:
Option A: Barium swallow
Used for esophageal disorders (achalasia, stricture, carcinoma). Shows esophagus and stomach, not rectum.
Option C: MRI defecogram
• Performed with MR sequences: shows pelvic floor musculature in detail. Not the X-ray-based contrast study shown here.
Option D: CT angiogram
Evaluates vascular anatomy and bleeding, not bowel evacuation.

stem: Identify the investigation performed in the image:
options: Single anastomosis with duodenal-ileal bypass with sleeve gastrectomy
subject: Surgery, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
The image shows: 1. Sleeve gastrectomy (removed portion of stomach visible).
2. Food passing through sleeve stomach -> duodeno-ileal single anastomosis. 3. Presence of biliopancreatic limb + common channel, typical of SADI-S procedure.
It is a newer bariatric/metabolic surgery combining restrictive (sleeve) + malabsorptive (duodeno-ileal bypass) elements.

Other Options:
Option A: Gastric bypass
In gastric bypass (Roux-en-Y), the food bypasses most of the stomach and duodenum with two anastomoses (gastro-jejunostomy + jejuno-jejunostomy).
Not seen here.
Option B: Sleeve gastrectomy
Only involves longitudinal resection of stomach -> no intestinal bypass component.
Not matching the figure.
Option C: Adjustable gastric banding
Involves a band around the stomach to restrict intake -> not seen in the diagram.

stem: A 32-year-old female presents with a breast lump, and mammography is suggestive of carcinoma breast. Her aunt had breast cancer and died at age 45 years. The patient's 29-year-old sister can afford only one screening test. Which screening test should be advised?
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations are the most important genetic risk factors for hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome.
• Strong family history, especially first- or second-degree relatives with breast cancer at young age (<50 years), warrants BRCA testing.
A single affordable test in this setting -> BRCA1 (and ideally BRCA2) testing.
Carriers have a 40-80% lifetime risk of breast cancer and increased risk of ovarian cancer.
• If mutation is detected -> requires enhanced surveillance, prophylactic mastectomy/oophorectomy, or chemoprevention.

Other Options:
Option A: CDH1
• Mutation associated with hereditary diffuse gastric cancer and lobular breast cancer, but less common.
Option B: Tp53
• Mutation seen in Li-Fraumeni syndrome -> multiple early-onset cancers (sarcomas, brain tumors, breast).
• Less likely as a single screening test in this case.
Option D: PTEN
• Mutation linked to Cowden syndrome (hamartomas, thyroid, breast, endometrial cancers).
• Not first-line in isolated strong family history of breast cancer.

stem: A 37-year-old unmarried nulliparous woman, having regular intercourse, is on oral contraceptive pills. Her mother was diagnosed with carcinoma breast at 60 years of age, and her elder sister was diagnosed with carcinoma ovary at 40 years of age. What is the next line of management?
options: Genetic counseling and screening for BRCA
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Strong family history of breast and ovarian cancer raises suspicion of Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer Syndrome (HBOC).
• BRCA1/BRCA2 testing is indicated as the next step. • Genetic counseling is essential before and after testing to explain implications for the patient and relatives.
• If BRCA-positive -> enhanced screening (MRI, mammography), risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy, and prophylactic mastectomy may be considered.

Other Options:
Option B: Routine mammography
• Mammography is a screening tool for average-risk women >40 years.
In high-risk women, genetic testing precedes imaging.
Option C: Stop taking oral contraceptive pills
• OCPs may slightly increase breast cancer risk, but they reduce ovarian cancer risk.
• Stopping OCPs is not the primary step in genetic risk evaluation.
Option D: Prophylactic surgery
Considered only after BRCA positivity is confirmed. • Not the immediate next step without genetic testing.

stem: Which of the following does NOT come under Breast Cancer T4b?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
T4b category of breast cancer (AJCC staging) includes skin involvement such as: 1. Ulceration of skin over breast 2. Ipsilateral edema / peau d'orange 3. Satellite skin nodules over the same breast. But erythema involving >1/3rd of the breast is classified as T4d (inflammatory breast carcinoma), not T4b.
Hence, "satellite nodules" are included in T4b, while erythema >1/3rd belongs to T4d.

Other Options:
Option A: Ulceration
• Included in T4b skin involvement.
Option B: Erythema >1/3
• Feature of T4d (inflammatory carcinoma), not T4b.
Option C: Peau d'orange
Classic sign of T4b due to lymphatic obstruction.

stem: What is the critical value of Ankle-Brachial Pressure Index (ABPI) below which gangrene is imminent?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• ABPI is used to assess peripheral arterial disease (PAD) severity: 1. 1.0-1.2: Normal 2. 0.9-1.0: Acceptable 3. 0.5-0.9: Claudication 4. <0.5: Severe ischemia, rest pain 5. <0.3: Critical ischemia -> gangrene imminent. • Thus, an ABPI below 0.3 indicates very poor distal perfusion, with high risk of ulceration, necrosis, and gangrene.

Other Options:
Option B: 0.5
• Suggests severe ischemia with rest pain, but not necessarily impending gangrene.
Option C: 1.2
• Normal or sometimes falsely elevated (e.g., arterial calcification in diabetes).
Option D: 0.9
• Borderline abnormal, usually associated with mild PAD/claudication.

stem: A 6-week-old baby presents with non-bilious, non-projectile vomiting for the past 3 weeks. On examination, the abdomen is unremarkable. What is the likely diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is the most likely cause in an infant with non-bilious, non-projectile vomiting and normal abdominal exam.
• Common in infants due to immature lower esophageal sphincter.
Vomiting is effortless, usually after feeds, and the child otherwise appears well.
• Management is mostly conservative (positioning, small frequent feeds, thickened feeds).

Other Options:
Option B: Milk allergy
• May cause vomiting, diarrhea, eczema, irritability, but often associated with systemic/allergic features, not isolated persistent vomiting.
Option C: Intestinal obstruction.
• Would typically cause bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, visible peristalsis, none of which are present here.
Option D: Pyloric stenosis
• Classic presentation is projectile non-bilious vomiting in a 3-6 week old baby, with palpable olive-shaped mass in the epigastrium.
Not consistent here since vomiting is non-projectile.

stem: A patient presents to triage after RTA with GCS 8/15. Focal neurological deficit is present. On examination, right-sided breath sounds are absent. What is the next step in management?
options: Secure IV line and start blood transfusion
subject: Surgery, Anesthesia
explanation:
Solution
In trauma, management follows the ATLS protocol (ABCDE).
• With a GCS of 8/15, the patient has a compromised airway -> Intubation is mandatory ("GCS <= 8 -> intubate"). Even though a chest injury (absent breath sounds -> likely pneumothorax/hemothorax) is present, the airway always comes first.
After securing the airway, breathing assessment follows (-> ICD placement if pneumothorax is confirmed).

Other Options:
Option A: CT head
Important for neurological evaluation but only after airway and breathing are secured.
Option B: Secure IV line and start blood transfusion
• Comes under Circulation (C), but must be done after airway (A) and breathing (B) are stabilized.
Option C: ICD
• Needed for pneumothorax/hemothorax, but it is a step after airway protection in this patient.

stem: The image shows a Z-Plasty procedure performed on the skin. What is the main use of this surgical technique?
options: Increasing the length of the scar
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
Z-Plasty is a plastic surgery technique used for: 1. Lengthening contracted scars (e.g., post-burn contractures).
2. Reorienting scars along natural skin creases (cosmetic camouflage). 3. Redistributing tension across scars to improve function and appearance.
• The procedure involves creating a Z-shaped incision, forming two triangular flaps, which are then transposed -> effectively increasing the functional length of the scar line.

Other Options:
Option B: Flap turning
Incorrect, though Z-Plasty uses flap transposition, its primary purpose is scar lengthening, not simple flap turning.
Option C: Cosmetic camouflage of scar
• Partially correct, but the primary indication is scar lengthening in contractures, with cosmetic benefit as a secondary effect.
Option D: Muscle release
• Not applicable; Z-Plasty is a skin procedure, not a muscle surgery.

stem: What among the following options is wrongly matched?
subject: Surgery, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Boston score is not related to colon cancer.
It is actually the Boston Bowel Preparation Scale (BBPS) -> used to grade quality of bowel preparation for colonoscopy, not staging or scoring colon cancer.

Other Options:
Option B: PTH-Miami criteria
Correct, Miami criteria are used intraoperatively during parathyroidectomy, based on PTH drop to confirm adequate excision.
Option C: GERD-los angles
Correct, Los Angeles classification grades GERD-related esophagitis (A-D) based on mucosal breaks seen in endoscopy.
Option D: Chronic pancreatitis - M Annheim
• Correct, M-ANNHEIM classification is widely used for chronic pancreatitis severity assessment.

stem: A 40 years old male presented to the emergency with severe respiratory distress with a blood pressure of 70/60 mmHg, trachea deviated to the right side with distended neck veins and absent breath sound on the left side. These findings are suggestive of tension pneumothorax. What is the most appropriate next step in the management?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening emergency that causes obstructive shock due to compression of great vessels.
First and immediate step = needle decompression (needle thoracostomy) -> usually in 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, or 5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line.
• Definitive management = Intercostal chest drain (ICD), but this follows decompression, not before.

Other Options:
Option B: ICD
• Correct as definitive treatment but not the first step; inserting ICD directly in tension pneumothorax without decompression risks worsening collapse.
Option C: CECT
Imaging has no role in suspected clinical diagnosis of tension pneumothorax -> delay is fatal.
Option D: Emergency thoracotomy
Indicated for massive hemothorax/penetrating trauma, not for simple tension pneumothorax.

stem: What is the investigation of choice for parathyroid adenoma?
subject: Surgery, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
Sestamibi (Tc-99m MIBI) scan is the gold standard/investigation of choice for localizing parathyroid adenomas.
• Works on the principle of increased mitochondrial activity in adenoma cells -> increased uptake & delayed washout. High sensitivity & specificity, used for preoperative localization before surgery.
• Non-invasive, functional imaging, preferred over anatomical scans.

Other Options:
Option B: 4D CT
• Useful when sestamibi scan is negative or equivocal.
• Provides detailed anatomical localization but not first-line.
Option C: CECT of neck
• Can show enlarged parathyroid, but has poor sensitivity compared to functional imaging.
• Not preferred as IOC.
Option D: FNAC
• Not recommended due to risk of fibrosis, seeding, and complications.
• Not used in diagnosis of parathyroid adenomas.

stem: Which of the following is not a contraindication for breast conservative surgery?
options: Persistent positive margins after repeat excision
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Tumour limited to a single quadrant is actually an indication for BCS (favorable scenario).
• BCS is offered when oncological safety + cosmetic outcome are possible.
• Absolute contraindications include previous chest wall radiation, persistent positive margins, pregnancy (relative), and certain collagen vascular diseases like scleroderma.

Other Options:
Option A: Scleroderma
Contraindication: Poor wound healing and radiation sensitivity -> poor cosmetic and functional results.
Option B: Previous radiation to chest wall
Contraindication: Cumulative radiation dose causes excessive tissue damage -> cannot safely re-radiate.
Option D: Persistent positive margins after repeat excision
• Contraindication: Residual tumour cannot be cleared with conservation -> mastectomy indicated.

stem: Which among the following is incorrect regarding negative pressure wound dressing?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• NPWT typically uses -75 to -125 mmHg negative pressure (sometimes -50 mmHg in fragile patients like elderly or graft sites).
• It helps remove exudate, reduce edema, promote angiogenesis, and increase granulation tissue proliferation.
10-20 mmHg is too low to be effective.

Other Options:
Option A: Dressing done after 5-7 days
• Correct: Dressings are usually changed every 3-7 days depending on wound exudate and infection status.
Option B: Increases proliferation of granulation tissue
Correct: NPWT stimulates angiogenesis, fibroblast proliferation, and granulation tissue growth.
Option C: Can be continuous or intermittent
• Correct: Therapy can be delivered either continuously (commonly used) or intermittently (to promote granulation).

stem: A 30-year-old female presented to the emergency department with burn injuries involving face, both upper limbs, and anterior chest. Calculate the Total Body Surface Area (TBSA) burnt according to Rule of 9:
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
Rule of 9 distribution: 1. Face (part of head) = 4.5% (since full head = 9%) 2. Each upper limb = 9% -> both = 18% 3. Anterior chest = 9% (half of anterior trunk = 18%) Total = 4.5 + 18 + 9 = 31.5% -> ~33% (range 33-36% acceptable).

Other Options:
Option B: 40-45%
• Overestimation; would require involvement of additional areas (like abdomen or posterior trunk).
Option C: 25-28%
• Underestimation; excludes part of burns already listed.
Option D: 50-55%
• Far too high; would mean half the body surface involved.

stem: A man falls on an outstretched hand. There is tenderness and swelling in the anatomical snuffbox. X-ray was inconclusive. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Glass holding cast and follow up 14 days
Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) Immediately
subject: Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Suspected scaphoid fracture with inconclusive X-ray -> best step is empirical immobilization in a thumb spica (glass-holding) cast and repeat X-ray after 10-14 days.
Scaphoid fracture is commonly missed initially, but delayed X-ray may show resorption and fracture line.
Early immobilization prevents AVN (avascular necrosis) and non-union, which are notorious complications of scaphoid fracture.

Other Options:
Option B: Urgent CT scan
CT is good for bony detail, but not mandatory as the first step in an acute setting if X-ray is negative but suspicion is high.
Option C: MRI-ligamentous injury
• MRI is highly sensitive for early occult scaphoid fracture, but in most exam scenarios, initial cast + follow-up X-ray is the answer.
Option D: Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) Immediately
• Not indicated unless fracture is displaced or confirmed on imaging.

stem: What is the investigation of choice for tuberculosis of the spine?
subject: Microbiology, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• CBNAAT (Cartridge-Based Nucleic Acid Amplification Test) is the investigation of choice for spinal TB.
It rapidly detects Mycobacterium tuberculosis DNA and provides information about rifampicin resistance within a few hours.
While X-ray/CT/MRI help in diagnosis & extent assessment, the definitive test for confirmation is microbiological -> CBNAAT (preferred over culture due to speed).

Other Options:
Option A: X ray
• Can show vertebral collapse, disc space narrowing, kyphosis (Pott's spine), but is not confirmatory.
Option C: Culture and sensitivity
• Gold standard for drug susceptibility, but it takes weeks, hence not the IOC in practice.
Option D: CT Scan
• Helpful for bony destruction and abscess localization, but not the investigation of choice.

stem: A 78-year-old patient presented with an intertrochanteric fracture (IT fracture). What is the treatment of choice?
subject: Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
Intertrochanteric (IT) fracture in the elderly is best managed with internal fixation if feasible.
• PFN is the gold standard for unstable intertrochanteric fractures and commonly used even in elderly due to: 1. Less blood loss compared to plating. 2. Biomechanically stable fixation. 3. Early mobilization prevents complications of prolonged bed rest.
• Hemiarthroplasty is considered in very elderly, osteoporotic, or medically unfit patients with severely comminuted fractures, but PFN remains preferred if fixation is possible.

Other Options:
Option A: Hemiarthroplasty
• Used mainly in intracapsular neck femur fractures in elderly, not first choice for intertrochanteric fractures.
Option B: Femoral Intramedullary nail
• Used in shaft femur fractures, not intertrochanteric.
Option D: Derotation boot
• Old conservative method, now obsolete due to high rates of complications (DVT, bedsores, pneumonia).

stem: A 70-year-old female presents with an extracapsular neck of femur fracture. What is the best step in management?
subject: Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Extracapsular fractures of the femoral neck = Intertrochanteric fractures.
• DHS (Dynamic Hip Screw) is the standard of care for stable intertrochanteric fractures in elderly patients.
• Allows controlled collapse at fracture site and provides stable fixation -> early mobilization, prevents complications of prolonged immobilization.

Other Options:
Option A: Interlocking plate
• Not preferred in IT fractures; plates have higher failure rates compared to DHS/PFN.
Option B: Russell's traction
Old conservative method, rarely used now. Leads to prolonged immobilization complications (bedsores, DVT, pneumonia).
Option C: Boot and bar cast
• Obsolete in adults; used sometimes in children's fractures but not for elderly hip fractures.

stem: A female patient with rheumatoid arthritis presents with cervical pain. What is the most likely cause?
subject: Orthopaedics, Rheumatology
explanation:
Solution
In rheumatoid arthritis, chronic synovitis leads to destruction of the transverse ligament of the atlas -> resulting in atlantoaxial subluxation.
Presents with cervical pain, instability, and neurological deficits if spinal cord compression occurs.
It is the most important cervical spine complication in RA and may require surgical stabilization.

Other Options:
Option B: Osteoporosis
• RA patients can have osteoporosis due to steroids, but it is not the primary cause of cervical pain.
Option C: Osteoarthritis
• A degenerative condition, not the cause of cervical pain in rheumatoid arthritis patients.
Option D: Cervical Canal Stenosis
Can occur in elderly due to degeneration, but in RA, the hallmark lesion is atlantoaxial subluxation, not stenosis.

stem: Deep branch of the ulnar nerve supplies all of the following muscles except:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Deep branch of ulnar nerve supplies all intrinsic hand muscles (except thenar muscles supplied by median nerve and 1st & 2nd lumbricals). It innervates -> hypothenar muscles (including opponens digiti minimi), 3rd & 4th lumbricals, palmar & dorsal interossei, adductor pollicis, and deep head of flexor pollicis brevis.
Flexor carpi ulnaris is supplied by the ulnar nerve itself (direct branch in forearm), not its deep branch.

Other Options:
Option A: Opponens digiti minimi
• Supplied by deep branch of ulnar nerve (hypothenar group).
Option B: 3rd lumbrical
1st & 2nd lumbricals -> median nerve; 3rd & 4th lumbricals -> deep branch of ulnar nerve.
Option C: Palmar or dorsal Interossei
• All interossei are supplied by the deep branch of the ulnar nerve.

stem: Which of the following vaccine cannot be given in a child with SCID?
subject: Pediatrics, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Children with Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID) should not receive live attenuated vaccines such as MMR, OPV, or varicella.
These can cause life-threatening infection.
• Tdap and Hepatitis B are inactivated vaccines -> safe.
IPV is also inactivated and safe. MMR is live attenuated -> contraindicated.

Other Options:
Option A: Tdap
Safe since it is an inactivated toxoid vaccine.
Option B: Hepatitis B
Recombinant subunit vaccine, safe even in immunodeficiency.
Option D: IPV
Killed vaccine, hence can be administered safely.

stem: A five-year-old presents with hepatosplenomegaly and cherry red spot. The child exhibits an abnormal startled response and developmental regression. Which enzyme is deficient?
subject: Pediatrics, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Tay-Sachs disease is caused by deficiency of Hexosaminidase A.
• Classic features include cherry red spot, neurodegeneration, startle response, and hepatosplenomegaly absent (differs from Niemann-Pick).

Other Options:
Option A: Sphingomyelinase
Deficiency -> Niemann-Pick disease, presents with hepatosplenomegaly + cherry red spot.
Option B: Glucocerebrosidase A
Deficiency -> Gaucher's disease (hepatosplenomegaly, bone crisis, crumpled macrophages).
Option C: Aryl sulfatase
Deficiency -> Metachromatic leukodystrophy, causes progressive demyelination, not cherry red spot.

stem:
Which of the following features indicate a proper breastfeeding position?
1. Chin touching the breast
2. Lower lip turned inwards
3. Upper part of areola is more visible than below
4. Baby cheeks are full and rounded
subject: Pediatrics, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Chin touching the breast indicates close attachment.
• Lower lip turned outwards (not inwards) is a sign of proper latch.
• Upper areola more visible confirms deeper lower jaw latch.
• Full, rounded cheeks show effective sucking and milk transfer.
• All four together confirm correct breastfeeding technique.

Other Options:
Option A: 1, 2, 3
Excludes cheek fullness, which is essential.
Option B: 1, 4
Incomplete; misses areolar asymmetry.
Option C: 1 & 3
Only partial signs, not sufficient.

stem: A baby delivered after 36 weeks of gestation presented with tachypnea in the first few hours of life the baby required O2 at 30% what is the most likely diagnosis:
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• TTNB is common in near-term/term infants.
Caused by delayed absorption of lung fluid.
Presents with tachypnea within hours of birth. Oxygen requirement is usually mild/moderate.
• Self-limiting, resolves in 48-72 hours.

Other Options:
Option B: Meconium aspiration syndrome
Seen in post-term, with meconium-stained fluid.
Option C: Respiratory distress syndrome
Occurs in preterm (<34 weeks), due to surfactant deficiency.
Option D: Pneumonia
Develops later, often with fever/sepsis features.

stem: A six-week-old infant presents with non-bilious, non-projectile vomiting since the 3rd week of life. Bowel movements are normal, abdominal radiographs are unremarkable. Likely diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
GERD in infants presents with non-bilious, effortless vomiting.
Growth usually normal, stools are passed normally.
No obstruction or metabolic derangement.
Common between 3 weeks to 3 months of age.
• Usually resolves spontaneously.

Other Options:
Option B: Pyloric stenosis
Causes projectile non-bilious vomiting with palpable olive.
Option C: Intestinal obstruction
Would cause bilious vomiting and distension.
Option D: Cow milk protein intolerance
Presents with diarrhea, blood in stools, not just vomiting.

stem: Reason for refeeding syndrome:
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Refeeding after starvation causes sudden carbohydrate intake.
• Leads to insulin surge -> drives phosphate, potassium, and magnesium into cells.
Results in hypophosphatemia, arrhythmias, and neurological problems.
Increased insulin is the central mechanism.
• Prevention: slow feeding with supplementation of electrolytes.

Other Options:
Option B: Decrease insulin
Seen in starvation state, not refeeding.
Option C: Both
Incorrect, only increased insulin.
Option D: None of the above
Incorrect, mechanism is established.

stem: Ten year old child is diagnosed with systemic lupus erythematosus along with nephrotic range of proteinuria renal biopsy revealed wire localization which of the following is the drug of choice:
subject: Pediatrics, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Wire loop lesions indicate Class IV lupus nephritis.
• Requires aggressive immunosuppression.
Combination of IV steroids + Cyclophosphamide is standard first-line therapy.
• Prevents renal progression.
• Mycophenolate can be alternative but not first-line here.

Other Options:
Option A: IV steroids
Monotherapy insufficient for severe lupus nephritis.
Option C: Mycophenolate mofetil
Used as maintenance or alternative in milder cases.
Option D: Cyclophosphamide only
Needs steroids in combination.

stem: A child presents with petite purpura, and platelet count of 10,000 cells per cubic millimeter bone marrow examination revealed increased megakaryocytes the next step is:
subject: Pediatrics, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Findings suggest Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP).
Severe thrombocytopenia (<20,000/mm3) with bleeding requires IVIG or steroids.
IVIG rapidly increases platelet count.
• Bone marrow is normal/increased megakaryocytes.
• Aspirin is contraindicated due to bleeding risk.

Other Options:
Option A: Bone marrow transplant
Not indicated in ITP.
Option B: Methotrexate
Not used for ITP management.
Option D: Aspirin
Worsens bleeding risk.

stem: What was not included in Turner syndrome:
subject: Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Turner syndrome (45,XO) classic features:
1. Short stature
2. Webbed neck
3. Shield chest with widely spaced nipples
4. Short 4th metacarpal
• Prominent occiput is a feature of Trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome), not Turner's.

Other Options:
Option A: Short 4th metacarpal
Characteristic of Turner's.
Option B: Widely spaced nipple
Classic shield chest.
Option C: Webbed neck
Classic feature due to cystic hygroma.

stem: A 9-month-old child cannot sit without support, but rolls over, says "Mama/Dada," and shows stranger anxiety. Next step?
options: Refer to Center for developmental assessment
Reassure as this is normal developmental variation
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
At 9 months, a child should sit without support.
• Rolling, babbling, and stranger anxiety are appropriate.
Isolated gross motor delay warrants further evaluation.
Referral helps identify cerebral palsy, neuromuscular disorders.

Other Options:
Option B: Reassure as this is normal developmental variation
Reassure - Delay is not normal variation.
Option C: Start physical therapy
Physical therapy - Not started without proper assessment.
Option D: Perform cranial MRI
Cranial MRI - Needed only if assessment suggests pathology.

stem: Which of the following can Causeway strider in neonate?
subject: Pediatrics, ENT
explanation:
Solution
Laryngomalacia is the commonest cause of neonatal stridor.
• Caused by floppy supraglottic structures.
Presents within weeks of birth, worsens when supine/crying.
Usually self-resolving by 18-24 months.
• Severe cases may need surgery (supraglottoplasty).

Other Options:
Option A: Subglottic stenosis
Less common, often post-intubation.
Option B: Hemorrhagic polyp
Rare, occurs in older children/adults.
Option D: Laryngeal papilloma
Viral etiology (HPV), not typical in neonates.

stem: When can you insert CUT 380A except?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, PSM
explanation:
Solution
Insertion during this phase risks implantation of an undetected early pregnancy because ovulation may have already occurred.
Hence, contraindicated.

Other Options:
Option A: Immediate post-partum
CUT 380A can be inserted within 10 minutes of placental delivery (post-placental insertion) under postpartum IUCD (PPIUCD) programs.
Option B: First 7 days of menses
This is the ideal (interval) time for CuT 380A insertion, ensuring the woman is not pregnant and insertion is easier due to natural cervical dilation.
Option D: Within 48hrs of delivery
• Can be done as part of PPIUCD insertion, though there is a slightly higher expulsion risk than interval insertion.

stem: Which of the following is not used for the treatment of PPH?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
Induction of labour is contraindicated in placenta previa, because the placenta obstructs the cervical os.
• Attempting induction can lead to massive antepartum or intrapartum hemorrhage, endangering both mother and fetus.
• Delivery in placenta previa is usually by cesarean section. Hence, placenta previa is the exclusion in this list.
All other options are valid indications for induction.

Other Options:
Option A: Post-term pregnancy
• Induction is recommended after 41 weeks, as risk of stillbirth and complications increases.
Option B: Severe preeclampsia
Induction or expedited delivery is often required to prevent maternal and fetal morbidity.
Option D: Intrauterine fetal death
• Induction of labour is indicated to deliver the fetus safely.

stem: Two doctor opinion on MTP. When is it needed?
subject: FMT, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• As per MTP Amendment Act 2021, up to 20 weeks: opinion of one registered medical practitioner is sufficient.
Between 20-24 weeks, the opinion of two doctors is required.

Other Options:
Option B: Less than 20
• Incorrect, only one doctor's opinion needed.
Option C: 12-16 weeks
Falls under <20 weeks category, so single doctor suffices.
Option D: Less than 12 weeks
Same as above, only one doctor required.

stem: Granulosa Cell tumor: Histopath Coffee bean nuclei. Gross specimen. The following tumor will show except:
subject: Pathology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
Granulosa cell tumor is a sex cord-stromal tumor of the ovary derived from granulosa cells.
These tumors are typically estrogen-secreting and often present with endometrial hyperplasia or postmenopausal bleeding.
Histologically, they show Call-Exner bodies (microfollicular pattern) and coffee-bean nuclei due to nuclear grooves.
Tumor markers: Inhibin and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) are positive, helping in diagnosis and follow-up.
CA-125 is not typically elevated in granulosa cell tumors; it is mainly associated with epithelial ovarian cancers.

Other Options:
Option B: Inhibin positive
True. Inhibin is a specific tumor marker for granulosa cell tumors.
Helps in diagnosis and recurrence monitoring.
Produced by granulosa cells under normal and neoplastic conditions.
Option C: Call-exner bodies
True. Characteristic histological finding showing rosette-like structures filled with eosinophilic fluid.
• Represents immature follicular differentiation.
Option D: Sex cord stroma tumor
True. Granulosa cell tumor belongs to the sex cord-stromal group, along with Sertoli-Leydig cell tumors and thecomas.
Arises from ovarian stroma derived from sex cords of the embryonic gonad.

stem: 30yr patient with infertility and irregular menses, Laparoscopic image: What is the diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Endometriosis is a common cause of infertility and irregular cycles, often visualized laparoscopically as chocolate cysts/implants.

Other Options:
Option B: Tubo-ovarian abscess
• Would present with fever, pain, infection signs.
Option C: Ovarian cyst
• Not typically associated with irregular cycles + classic laparoscopy findings.
Option D: PCOD siterus
• Diagnosed by ultrasound/clinical features, not laparoscopy.

stem: Female, first trimester, urine analysis done. Culture shows E.coli. Which of the following is the complication of this? Single best answer.
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
E. coli is the most common cause of asymptomatic bacteriuria and urinary tract infections in pregnancy.
If untreated, bacteriuria can progress to pyelonephritis, which is strongly associated with preterm labour and low birth weight.
Inflammatory cytokines and maternal systemic response can trigger uterine contractions, leading to premature delivery.
• Early detection and antibiotic therapy reduce the risk of complications significantly.
• Hence, preterm labour is the most important and well-documented complication.

Other Options:
Option A: Miscarriage
• Not the classical complication of E.coli UTI in early pregnancy.
• Miscarriage is multifactorial, but asymptomatic bacteriuria is not a major cause.
Option C: Fetal Death
Rare in isolated E. coli bacteriuria; occurs only if infection becomes systemic and severe.
• Not considered a primary complication.
Option D: Chorioamnionitis
Typically associated with prolonged rupture of membranes or ascending infection in late pregnancy.
• Not the main complication of first trimester E. coli bacteriuria.

stem: Modified Rotterdam Criteria includes which of the following?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
The Modified Rotterdam Criteria (2003) is used for the diagnosis of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS).
• According to the criteria, 2 out of 3 features must be present:
1. Oligo- or anovulation (abnormal menses).
2. Clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism (e.g. hirsutism, acne, elevated androgens).
3. Polycystic ovaries on ultrasound (>= 12 follicles in either ovary or ovarian volume >10 cm3).
• Hence, abnormal menses represents one of the diagnostic components.
• Obesity and insulin resistance are common associations but not diagnostic criteria.

Other Options:
Option A: Obesity
Commonly associated with PCOS but not part of the diagnostic criteria.
Seen in about 50% of women with PCOS due to metabolic dysfunction.
Option B: Hirsutism
• Reflects clinical hyperandrogenism, which is one of the three diagnostic features.
• However, the question asks for a single best answer and abnormal menses directly represents a diagnostic criterion.
Option D: Insulin Resistance
• A pathophysiological feature, not included in the Rotterdam diagnostic criteria.
• Plays a role in metabolic manifestations but not diagnostic confirmation.

stem: Female, wants to conceive and has seizure disorder. Which is the most teratogenic drug which you wont prescribe due to risk of Neural tube defects?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Valproate is the most teratogenic antiepileptic drug and is strongly associated with neural tube defects (NTDs) like spina bifida.
Teratogenicity is dose-dependent, with risk increasing at doses >1000 mg/day.
Other associated risks include cardiac defects, cleft lip/palate and cognitive impairment in exposed fetuses.
Because of these risks, valproate is contraindicated in women planning pregnancy unless no other drug controls seizures.
• Safer alternatives like lamotrigine and levetiracetam are generally preferred in pregnancy.

Other Options:
Option B: Oxcarbazepine
• Structurally related to carbamazepine but less teratogenic.
• Rarely associated with NTDs compared to valproate.
Option C: Lamotrigine
• Considered one of the safest AEDs in pregnancy.
• Minimal risk of major malformations.
Option D: Levitiracetam
• Also relatively safe in pregnancy.
• No significant association with congenital malformations.

stem: Fetal Bradycardia during labour is seen in all except:
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Maternal fever is more commonly associated with fetal tachycardia, not bradycardia.
• Causes include intrauterine infections (chorioamnionitis), epidural analgesia or maternal sepsis.
Hence, maternal fever produces increased baseline heart rate, not decreased. This makes it the correct exclusion among the given choices.
• Recognizing these distinctions is key during intrapartum fetal monitoring.

Other Options:
Option B: Head compression
• Can cause reflex vagal stimulation in the fetus.
• Results in transient bradycardia during labour.
Option C: Abruption Placenta
Leads to fetal hypoxia due to compromised placental circulation.
• Bradycardia is a common manifestation of severe abruption.
Option D: Congenital heart block
• Causes persistent fetal bradycardia on antenatal monitoring.
• Often associated with maternal autoimmune diseases (e.g. SLE, Sjögren's).

stem: According to the Modified Rotterdam Criteria, which of the following is one of the diagnostic features of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• The Modified Rotterdam Criteria (2003) define PCOS when two of the following three features are present:
1. Oligo- or anovulation (abnormal or infrequent menses)
2. Clinical or biochemical signs of hyperandrogenism
3. Polycystic ovaries on ultrasound (>= 12 follicles or ovarian volume >10 cm3)
Oligo- or anovulation leading to irregular or absent menstrual cycles is therefore a key diagnostic criterion. • Other factors like obesity and insulin resistance are associated findings but not diagnostic.

Other Options:
Option A: Obesity
• Frequently associated with PCOS but not a diagnostic criterion. Represents a metabolic feature rather than a diagnostic parameter.
Option B: Insulin resistance
• Plays a major pathophysiological role, but not included in the diagnostic definition. Commonly seen but not required for diagnosis.
Option D: Hyperinsulinemia
Reflects insulin resistance, an associated metabolic abnormality.
• Not part of the Rotterdam diagnostic framework.

stem: Cervical cancer, which HPV is most common?
subject: Pathology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
HPV 16 and 18 are responsible for approximately 70% of cervical cancer cases worldwide.
• They are classified as high-risk oncogenic strains due to their strong association with cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN) and invasive cancer.
Persistent infection with HPV 16/18 disrupts E6/E7 tumor suppressor pathways, leading to malignant transformation.
• Vaccines (bivalent, quadrivalent and nonavalent) are designed to protect against HPV 16 and 18 specifically.
• Thus, they are the most significant causative subtypes for cervical carcinoma.

Other Options:
Option B: 6,11
• Classified as low-risk HPV types. • Cause genital warts, not cervical cancer.
Option C: 31,33
• High-risk strains but contribute to a smaller percentage of cervical cancers compared to 16/18. Usually implicated in co-infection cases.
Option D: 43
• Not associated with cervical cancer. • Considered a low-risk, non-oncogenic type.

stem: Multiloculated, heterogenous, ascites, adnexal mass, CA 125 Increased, omental cake:
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Serous cystadenocarcinoma is the most common malignant ovarian tumor.
• It typically presents with ascites, omental caking and raised CA-125 levels.
Imaging shows heterogenous, multiloculated adnexal mass with papillary projections. Spread is often by peritoneal seeding, explaining ascites and omental deposits.
• Hence, the described features strongly indicate serous ovarian carcinoma.

Other Options:
Option B: Mucinous
• Can be large and multiloculated but less commonly associated with omental caking.
• More often linked with pseudomyxoma peritonei.
Option C: Endometrial cancer
• Primarily arises from endometrium of uterus, not adnexa.
• Does not typically present with CA-125 elevation and omental caking.
Option D: Endometrioid
• Ovarian endometrioid carcinoma is less common.
• Often associated with endometriosis, not classic for ascites + omental cake.

stem: Which of the following is false about Turner's syndrome?
subject: Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Turner's syndrome (45, XO) is characterized by short stature, streak ovaries and multiple somatic anomalies.
• Typical skeletal features include short 4th metacarpal and cubitus valgus.
Patients often have widely spaced nipples due to shield chest and a webbed neck from congenital lymphedema.
Prominent occiput is not a feature of Turner's syndrome; instead, it is seen in Trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome).
Thus, this option is false in the context of Turner's syndrome.

Other Options:
Option A: Short 4th metacarpal
• Classical skeletal feature in Turner's syndrome. • Helps in clinical diagnosis.
Option C: Widely spaced nipples
• Due to shield chest deformity. A common exam-related feature.
Option D: Webbed neck
• Results from cystic hygroma and congenital lymphedema. • Characteristic dysmorphic feature in Turner's syndrome.

stem: Which of the following is not an aneuploidy?
subject: Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Aneuploidy is defined as the presence of an abnormal number of chromosomes (extra or missing).
• Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY), Trisomy 13 (Patau's syndrome) and Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) are all caused by chromosomal aneuploidy.
Bloom syndrome, however, is a rare autosomal recessive disorder due to mutation in the BLM gene (DNA helicase defect), not an aneuploidy.
• It presents with short stature, photosensitivity, immunodeficiency and cancer predisposition. • Hence, Bloom syndrome is not classified under aneuploidy.

Other Options:
Option A: Klinefelter syndrome
• Results from extra X chromosome (47,XXY).
• A classical example of sex chromosome aneuploidy.
Option C: Trisomy 13
Caused by an extra chromosome 13. • Known as Patau syndrome, a type of autosomal aneuploidy.
Option D: Trisomy 21
• Caused by an extra chromosome 21. • The most common autosomal aneuploidy, i.e., Down syndrome.
